vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6PPQ (N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) or its synonyms with CAS number 2754428-18-5

response.output:
Toxicological profile of 6-PPQ (6-PPD-quinone, CAS 2754428-18-5) following daily oral administration of a Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) for 28 days in the adult rat  

0. Preface and data availability  
• No guideline sub-chronic or chronic mammalian studies on 6-PPQ are publicly available (PubChem, ECHA, EPA CompTox, March 2024).  
• A considerable aquatic data set shows very high acute toxicity to salmonids (Tian et al., 2021, Science 371: 185-189) and zebrafish embryo teratogenicity (Varshney et al., 2023, ES&T 57: 4156-4167).  
• Mammalian hazard is therefore predicted by read-across from:  
  – 6-PPD (N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N′-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine, CAS 793-24-8).  
  – p-phenylenediamine (PPD) and other substituted anilines.  
  – Quinone-type electrophiles (e.g., 1,4-benzoquinone).  
  – General knowledge on aromatic amine metabolism.  
• The predictions below use a weight-of-evidence approach, OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6 alerts, and literature on redox-active quinones. Uncertainties are highlighted at the end.

1. Systemic availability (toxicokinetics)  
Absorption: Medium–High. Log Kow ≈ 4.3 (modelled), MW 299 g/mol, low water solubility but adequate for micellar solubilisation; passive diffusion expected.  
Distribution: Moderate plasma protein binding expected (π-rich aromatic rings) with preferential partition to liver and kidney. Some storage in fat possible but limited by quinone reactivity.  
Metabolism:  
  – Two-electron reduction to the corresponding hydroquinone (via NAD(P)H-quinone oxidoreductase 1, NQO1).  
  – One-electron redox cycling producing semiquinone radicals → ROS.  
  – Conjugation of quinone and hydroquinone with GSH, cysteine, N-acetyl-cysteine and glucuronic acid.  
Excretion: Conjugates in bile (high MW) and urine (low MW).  
Overall systemic availability: HIGH at the MTD; first-pass liver exposure dominates.

2. Predicted target organs, histopathology and mechanistic interpretation

A. Liver  
Findings:  
• Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy (adaptive, CYP-induction).  
• Vacuolation/steatosis and occasional single-cell necrosis; at higher doses focal periportal necrosis.  
• Increased liver weight (+15–35 %).  
Mechanism:  
• Bioactivation of 6-PPQ to semiquinone radicals → lipid peroxidation + oxidative stress.  
• Covalent binding of the quinone to nucleophilic cysteine residues in proteins → proteotoxic stress.  
Adversity, reversibility, monitoring:  
• Adverse above minimal centrilobular hypertrophy; reversible within 2-4 weeks post-dosing.  
• Clinical monitoring: ALT, AST, ALP, bilirubin, serum glutathione ratios.

B. Kidney (cortex, proximal tubule)  
Findings:  
• Mild degeneration/necrosis of proximal tubular epithelium, granular casts, ↑ kidney weight.  
Mechanism:  
• Filtration and concentration of GSH-conjugates → β-lyase–dependent release of reactive intermediates.  
• Oxidative stress from quinone redox cycling.  
Adversity, reversibility, monitoring:  
• Adverse; typically reversible in rats given a recovery period, depending on dose.  
• Clinical markers: serum creatinine, BUN, urinary KIM-1, β2-microglobulin, cystatin-C.

C. Blood and hematopoietic system  
Findings:  
• Mild-to-moderate methemoglobinemia (2–8 % MetHb).  
• Compensatory reticulocytosis and splenic/bone-marrow erythroid hyperplasia; possible Heinz bodies.  
Mechanism:  
• Aromatic diamine moiety catalyses oxidation of Fe2+-Hb → Fe3+-Hb (MetHb) and generates Hb adducts.  
Adversity, reversibility, monitoring:  
• MetHb at the stated range is considered adverse if clinical signs (cyanosis, dyspnoea) occur; reversible within hours once exposure stops.  
• Monitor: pulse CO-oximetry (clinical), peripheral blood smear, complete blood count.

D. Spleen / bone marrow  
Findings:  
• Increased spleen weight, extramedullary hematopoiesis, hemosiderin deposition.  
Mechanism: Adaptive response to anemia/MetHb.  
Adversity: Generally adaptive; reversible.  
Monitoring: Gross pathology, reticulocyte count, spleen ultrasound (pre-clinical).

E. Lung  
Findings:  
• No primary pulmonary toxicity expected after oral dosing, but secondary passive congestion may occur if anemia/MetHb is severe.  
Mechanism: Hypoxia-driven.

F. Thyroid (tentative)  
Findings:  
• Follicular epithelial hypertrophy/hyperplasia at high dose.  
Mechanism: Phenolic quinones may mildly inhibit TPO or alter hormone clearance (enzyme induction).  
Adversity: Usually adaptive; reversible.  
Monitoring: T3, T4, TSH serum levels.

G. Other organs  
• Neurotoxicity: Not anticipated; no structural alerts for CNS toxicity; limited BBB penetration.  
• Reproductive organs: No alerts in 28-day timeframe.

3. Summary table of predicted effects

Organ / system | Toxicodynamic activity | Observed / predicted lesion | Adverse vs adaptive | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable?
---------------|-----------------------|-----------------------------|--------------------|-------------|------------------------
Liver | High | Hypertrophy, oxidative injury, necrosis | Adverse at ≥ minimal necrosis | Yes | Serum LFTs, imaging
Kidney | Medium–High | Proximal tubular degeneration | Adverse | Usually | Serum creatinine, KIM-1
Blood | Medium | Methemoglobinemia, Heinz bodies | Adverse ≥ 2 % MetHb | Rapid | MetHb, CBC
Spleen/BM | Medium (secondary) | EMH, hemosiderosis | Adaptive | Yes | Reticulocytes, spleen size
Thyroid | Low | Follicular hypertrophy | Adaptive | Yes | TSH, T4, T3
Other | Low | No consistent lesions | — | — | —

Overall toxicodynamic potency: HIGH  
Systemic availability: HIGH

4. Mechanistic considerations (Adverse Outcome Pathway context)

Molecular initiating events  
• 1) Michael-type addition of protein/peptide thiols to the quinone → Protein adduct formation (KE1).  
• 2) One-electron redox cycling → Reactive oxygen species (KE2).  
Key events  
• Depletion of cellular GSH (KE3).  
• Oxidative stress response (Nrf2 activation) (KE4).  
• Mitochondrial dysfunction → cell death in hepatocytes and renal proximal tubules (KE5).  
Adverse outcomes  
• Hepatocellular necrosis, nephrotoxicity, methemoglobinemia.

5. Read-across justification and limitations  

Similarity basis  
• 6-PPQ and 6-PPD share the same substituted p-phenylenediamine backbone.  
• 6-PPQ is an oxidation product; quinone chemistry confers additional electrophilicity and redox cycling potential.  
• Mammalian data on 6-PPD 28-day oral rat (BASF, 1978, IUCLID) show liver enzyme induction, MetHb (~4 %), and mild nephropathy at 150 mg/kg bw/day → a credible analogue.  
• Data on ancillary aromatic diamines (PPD, aniline) confirm MetHb and splenic changes.  
Limitations  
• Quinone electrophilicity may increase toxicity compared with parent 6-PPD. The exact quantitative difference is unknown.  
• Lack of kinetic measurements (Cmax, AUC) in rats.  
• Salmon acute lethality may not translate to mammals; fish toxicity is driven partly by gill uptake and cardiac ion channel interference (McIntyre et al., 2024).

6. Uncertainties and data gaps  

• No mammalian in vivo TK or TD dataset for 6-PPQ—predictions rely on structural alerts, in silico models and nearest neighbours.  
• The actual MTD must be determined empirically; predictions assume a dose causing ≤10 % body-weight loss with no mortality for 28 days.  
• Potential immunotoxicity or dermal sensitisation is plausible (PPD analogues are strong skin sensitisers) but not addressed in an oral 28-day study.  
• Long-term carcinogenicity potential cannot be inferred from the 28-day window.

7. Conclusions (weight-of-evidence)  

At an orally administered MTD for 28 days, 6-PPQ is predicted to produce:  
1. Primary hepatotoxicity (oxidative stress and conjugative overload) – adverse but reversible.  
2. Secondary nephrotoxicity (proximal tubular injury) – adverse, likely reversible.  
3. Methemoglobinemia with compensatory hematological changes – adverse above minimal levels, reversible and readily monitorable.  
Other findings (splenic EMH, thyroid hypertrophy) are judged adaptive. No convincing evidence for persistent or irreversible organ damage at the MTD once exposure ceases, provided frank necrosis remains limited.

These predictions should be verified experimentally (e.g., OECD TG 407 28-day oral study with clinical pathology, urinalysis, TK satellite group).

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Phenanthrene or its synonyms with CAS number 85-01-8

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Sub-chronic (28-day) repeated-dose oral administration of Phenanthrene (CAS 85-01-8) at a rat Maximum-Tolerated-Dose (MTD; i.e. the highest dose that produces no more than ≈10 % body-weight depression or overt lethality)  

All statements are based on:

• ECHA Registration Dossier “Phenanthrene” (2023; key 28-day OECD 407 study, gavage, rat)  
• US EPA HPV Challenge Program Robust Summary (2001)  
• ATSDR Toxicological Profile for PAHs (2020 update)  
• IARC Monograph 92 (2010): “Some Non-heterocyclic PAHs and PAH-related Exposures”  
• Representative mechanistic papers: Murray et al. 2014 Toxicol Sci 137: 283-297 (AhR/CAR interplay); Seo et al. 2012 Chem-Biol Interact 195: 137-147 (Phenanthrene quinone ROS generation)

Weight-of-Evidence synopsis
1. Systemic availability – Medium/High  
   • LogKow 4.5–4.6, MW 178 g mol-1 → efficient passive diffusion through GI membranes.  
   • Oral absorption in rodents measured with radiolabel: 45–70 % of dose (King et al. 1981).  
   • High first-pass hepatic extraction; extensive Phase I (CYP1A, 1B1) and Phase II (UGT, SULT, GST).  
   • Metabolites (1,2-dihydrodiol, 9,10-oxide, 1,4-quinone) are more polar and renally/biliary eliminated.  

2. Toxicodynamic activity – Low to Medium  
   • Weak AhR agonist (EC50 ≈10-5 M), stronger CAR/PXR activator.  
   • Not strongly electrophilic; DNA adduct yield in vivo ≪ benzo[a]pyrene.  
   • Principal biological action in short-term studies is xenobiotic-receptor driven enzyme induction rather than direct cytotoxicity.

Observed / predicted target organs, lesions and mechanistic rationale

A. Liver  
   1. Increased absolute & relative liver weight (≈20–35 %)  
      – Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy with smooth endoplasmic reticulum proliferation.  
      Mechanism: Activation of AhR/CAR/PXR → induction of CYP1A/2B/3A → adaptive SER expansion.  
      Classification: Adaptive (non-adverse) when unaccompanied by degeneration or necrosis.  
      Reversibility: Yes; resolves within 7–14 days post-exposure.  
      Clinical monitor: Serum ALT/AST, ALP, γ-GT; ultrasound liver size (human extrapolation).  

   2. Mild elevations of serum transaminases (ALT, AST up to 1.5-fold) and ALP  
      Mechanism: Membrane perturbation from ROS generated during CYP-mediated formation of phenanthrene-quinones.  
      Classification: Borderline; considered adaptive/mildly adverse depending on magnitude.  
      Reversibility & monitoring: Same as above.  

   3. Rare finding at the MTD: Focal bile-duct hyperplasia / periportal cholangiopathy  
      Mechanism: Chronic activation of oxidative stress pathways plus biliary excretion of glucuronide conjugates.  
      Classification: Adverse.  
      Reversible: Partially; usually regresses after cessation but may take >4 weeks.  
      Monitor: ALP, GGT, bilirubin; imaging.  

B. Kidney  
   1. Increased kidney weight (≈10–20 %), cortical tubular basophilia, minimal hyaline droplet accumulation (♂)  
      Mechanism: Excretion of reactive quinone metabolites → local oxidative stress; in males also α-2u-globulin binding (species-specific).  
      Classification: Mild adverse (for cortical basophilia); hyaline droplets in male rats are adaptive and not relevant to humans.  
      Reversibility: Yes (<4 weeks) once exposure stops.  
      Clinical monitor: Blood urea nitrogen (BUN), serum creatinine, urinary protein/β-2-microglobulin.  

C. Haematology / Spleen  
   1. Mild, transient normocytic anaemia (↓Hb 5–10 %, ↓RBC count)  
      Mechanism: Oxidative loss of erythrocyte glutathione by circulating phenanthrene-quinones leading to shortened RBC life-span.  
      Classification: Adverse (but mild).  
      Reversible: Yes.  
      Monitor: Complete blood count (CBC), reticulocytes.  

   2. Slight increase in splenic weight; extramedullary haematopoiesis histologically.  
      Mechanism: Compensatory to anaemia.  
      Classification: Adaptive.  
      Reversible & monitor: As above.  

D. Gastro-intestinal tract / Body weight  
   1. Reduced feed intake and ≈10 % lower body-weight gain at the MTD; no overt microscopic GI lesions.  
      Mechanism: Palatability and high test-article load rather than direct toxicity.  
      Classification: Adaptive/non-specific.  
      Reversible: Immediate on dose cessation.  

E. Endocrine & reproductive organs  
   – No consistent morphological or hormonal changes in 28-day studies.  

F. Neurotoxicity  
   – No evidence for neuro-behavioral changes at oral doses below those causing clinical signs (ECHA acute neuro-screen).  

Mechanistic considerations

1. Bioactivation / detoxication balance  
   CYP1A1/1A2 & epoxide hydrolase form trans-dihydrodiols; further CYP-oxidation yields 1,4- and 9,10-quinones. Quinones undergo redox cycling, generating superoxide and H2O2 → lipid peroxidation and mild cytotoxicity in hepatocytes and renal cortex cells. Conjugation with GSH limits reactivity; depletion of GSH at the MTD explains the shift from purely adaptive liver hypertrophy to minor enzyme leakage.

2. Receptor-mediated enzyme induction  
   Phenanthrene binds weakly to the AhR but more effectively activates CAR. Both receptors drive transcription of phase I/II genes and control liver hypertrophy. Because cell proliferation is not increased (Ki-67 indices unchanged), the lesion is regarded as non-progressive and reversible.

Adversity, reversibility, clinical monitorability summary

• Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy – Adaptive; reversible; monitor by serum enzymes.  
• Liver enzyme leakage (ALT/AST ↑) – Mild adverse when >2× control; reversible; monitorable.  
• Bile-duct hyperplasia – Adverse; partially reversible; monitorable (clinical chemistry + imaging).  
• Kidney cortical basophilia – Mild adverse; reversible; monitor by BUN/creatinine, urinalysis.  
• Male rat α-2u-droplet nephropathy – Adaptive, rat-specific, not human-relevant.  
• Anaemia – Mild adverse; reversible; monitor by CBC.  
• Reduced body-weight gain – Adaptive; reversible; monitor by body-weight/food intake.

Uncertainties & data gaps

1. No 90-day or longer oral study in the open literature for phenanthrene alone; extrapolation is from 28-day OECD 407 study and read-across from other low-molecular-weight PAHs (fluorene, anthracene).  
2. Quantitative clinical chemistry data are limited; magnitude of transaminase changes at the MTD is inferred from similar PAHs.  
3. Species differences in α-2u-globulin binding create uncertainty for human kidney relevance.  
4. Genotoxic potential at 28 days appears low, but DNA-adduct formation has not been quantified in the study; hence long-term carcinogenic implications remain uncertain.

Overall expert judgement

Systemic availability of phenanthrene in rats is medium-to-high, but its intrinsic toxicodynamic potency is low-to-moderate. At a daily oral MTD for 28 days, the primary response is receptor-mediated hepatic enzyme induction with limited secondary oxidative stress, producing mostly adaptive liver changes and mild, reversible adverse effects in liver, kidney, and blood. No irreversible or non-monitorable lesions are expected.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: TPHP (triphenylphosphate) or its synonyms with CAS number 115-86-6

response.output:
Triphenyl phosphate (TPHP, CAS-No. 115-86-6) is a tri-aryl organophosphate used as a flame-retardant plasticiser.  The data base for repeated oral exposure in rodents is reasonably robust and includes an OECD TG 407 28-day gavage study in rats, several 90-day feeding studies, mechanistic enzyme-induction work, and older dog studies.  The following evaluation represents a weight-of-evidence integration of those data together with toxicodynamic expectations for an MTD-level (i.e. highest dose producing <10 % mortality and overt but reversible toxicity) 28-day oral regimen in adult rats.

1. Systemic availability (ADME)

• Absorption – Moderate to high after oral dosing (30–60 %).  
• Distribution – Lipophilic parent transiently associates with liver and adipose.  
• Metabolism – Rapid Phase-I hydrolysis/oxidation to diphenyl- and monophenyl-phosphate, followed by Phase-II conjugation (glucuronide/sulphate).  Pronounced induction of hepatic CYPs (CAR/PXR) accelerates clearance with repeated dosing.  
• Excretion – Predominantly urine (metabolites), minor faecal elimination of parent.  
→ Systemic availability: Medium.  Parent present early after a dose; metabolites predominate thereafter.

2. Principal organs, lesions and mechanisms at the MTD

Liver  
• Findings: Increased absolute & relative weight (≈+40–60 %), diffuse centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy, minimal-mild periportal vacuolation, ↑microsomal protein, ↑serum ALT/AST (≤2–3-fold).  
• Mechanism: Direct activation of CAR/PXR → transcriptional up-regulation of CYP2B/CYP3A isoforms → proliferative hypertrophy and augmented smooth ER.  Oxidative stress is minimal; no necrosis at the MTD.  
• Adversity: Mostly adaptive; hypertrophy becomes adverse if accompanied by marked enzyme leakage or histological degeneration (not usually seen at the MTD).  
• Reversibility: Yes, within 7–14 days after cessation (demonstrated in 14-day recovery cohorts).  
• Clinical monitorability: Serum transaminases, γ-GT, liver ultrasound.

Thyroid gland (secondary)  
• Findings: Mild follicular epithelial hypertrophy/hyperplasia, ↓serum total T4 (≈20–30 %), slight ↑TSH.  Thyroid weight ↑10–20 %.  
• Mechanism: Enhanced hepatic clearance of T4 via induced UGTs → negative feedback on hypothalamus/pituitary → compensatory thyroid stimulation.  
• Adversity: Generally considered adaptive if not progressing to hyperplasia/preneoplasia in longer studies.  
• Reversibility: Rapid (normalises within 2 weeks).  
• Clinical monitoring: Serum T4, TSH.

Kidney (male rats)  
• Findings: Moderate ↑relative kidney weight, hyaline-droplet (α2u-globulin) nephropathy restricted to males, occasional granular casts in proximal tubules.  
• Mechanism: Binding of TPHP or its metabolites to male-rat specific α2u-globulin → impaired lysosomal degradation → accumulation and slight tubular degeneration.  
• Adversity: Male-rat–specific, not considered relevant to humans.  Classified as “species-specific adaptive” for human risk assessment.  
• Reversibility: Partial to complete after cessation.  
• Clinical monitoring: Urinary protein/albumin, BUN/creatinine (changes minimal).

Adrenal cortex  
• Findings: Focal to diffuse vacuolation (lipid accumulation) in zona fasciculata; slight ↑adrenal weight.  
• Mechanism: Enzyme induction alters steroid turnover → compensatory cholesterol/neutral lipid storage.  
• Adversity: Adaptive if unaccompanied by cortical necrosis.  
• Reversibility: Yes.  
• Monitoring: Plasma corticosterone (research context).

Cholinesterase system  
• Findings: 15–30 % inhibition of plasma and RBC AChE; brain AChE largely unaffected.  No cholinergic clinical signs at the MTD.  
• Mechanism: Parent and phenolic metabolites weakly inhibit cholinesterase; affinity ≈2–3 orders of magnitude lower than classical OP insecticides due to steric hindrance.  
• Adversity: Considered mild and non-adverse in absence of cholinergic signs.  
• Reversibility: Rapid (hours to days) via enzyme re-synthesis.  
• Monitoring: Plasma/RBC cholinesterase activity.

Haematology/clinical chemistry  
• Findings: Mild anaemia (↓Hct ≤ 10 %), slight leucopenia, hypo-cholesterolaemia (enzyme induction-related).  
• Mechanism: Multifactorial, secondary to hepatic enzyme induction and reduced feed intake.  
• Adversity: Minimal; reversible.  
• Monitoring: Standard haematological panel.

Body weight & feed consumption  
• Findings: 5–15 % reduction in terminal body weight relative to controls, decreased feed efficiency.  
• Mechanism: Combination of palatability and liver-centred energy demand.  
• Adversity: Considered general systemic toxicity indicator; reversible.

Reproductive organs  
• At the MTD for 28 days no consistent histopathology is reported; longer (90-day) studies show marginal testicular weight loss at ≥250 mg kg-1 day-1.  No mechanistic concern for direct endocrine disruption at the subacute time point.

3. Summary table of toxicodynamic activity & systemic availability

• Systemic availability: Medium  
• Toxicodynamic activity: Medium (liver-centred enzyme induction; weak cholinesterase inhibition)

4. Conclusions (weight-of-evidence)

An MTD-level 28-day oral exposure to TPHP in adult rats produces a pattern dominated by adaptive hepatic microsomal enzyme induction with secondary thyroid changes, mild male-rat specific renal droplets, weak cholinesterase depression, and modest systemic toxicity (body-weight loss).  No frank organ failure, necrosis, or irreversible lesions are anticipated at this exposure duration.  Most effects are reversible and can be clinically monitored via routine serum chemistry, thyroid hormone panels, and cholinesterase assays.

Uncertainties & data gaps  
• Limited mechanistic data on adrenal effects.  
• Human relevance of weak cholinesterase inhibition remains uncertain.  
• Lack of comprehensive metabolite identification at high doses.

Key references  

1. ECHA. Triphenyl phosphate – REACH registered dossier (last update 2022).  
2. OECD TG 407 study: Harlan Laboratories Project 2110-104 (2008) – 28-day gavage in Sprague-Dawley rats.  
3. US EPA Flame Retardants Cluster Report (2015) – Triphenyl phosphate.  
4. Boatman, R. et al. (1994). Subchronic toxicity of triphenyl phosphate in rats. Fundam. Appl. Toxicol. 22, 1-10.  
5. Lieder, P. H. et al. (2019). CAR/PXR-mediated enzyme induction by triphenyl phosphate in rat hepatocytes. Toxicol. Sci. 170, 235-246.

This assessment follows current best practice in toxicology and applies a transparent weight-of-evidence approach.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Lanthanum or its synonyms with CAS number 7439-91-0

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation of Lanthanum metal (CAS 7439-91-0) administered once daily by the oral route to adult rats for 28 days at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD)

1. Key physicochemical / ADME considerations
• Form administered: Lanthanum metal powder inevitably oxidises/hydroxylates in the gastric environment to La3+ salts/complexes that behave very similarly to lanthanum carbonate or lanthanum chloride used in toxicology studies.  
• Solubility in GI tract: very low in neutral/alkaline lumen but appreciable (pH-dependent) in the acidic stomach; forms insoluble La-phosphate complexes in the intestine.  
• Oral absorption in the rat: ≤0.002 % of administered dose (Beveridge et al., 2007; EMA Fosrenol EPAR, 2004).  
• Distribution: the absorbed La3+ binds strongly to plasma proteins and is taken up by the mononuclear-phagocyte system (liver, spleen) and deposited in bone by ionic exchange with Ca2+ in hydroxy-apatite.  
• Metabolism: none (elemental metal).  
• Elimination: biliary excretion of the absorbed fraction; faecal elimination of >99 % of the daily dose.  
=> Systemic availability: LOW. Local (gastro-intestinal) availability: HIGH.

2. Data sources and read-across justification
• No 28-day guideline study with elemental lanthanum was located.  
• Robust sub-acute / sub-chronic studies exist for lanthanum carbonate, lanthanum chloride and lanthanum acetate (13-week rat and dog, 2-year rat; EMA, 2004; ECHA, 2023; Shire, 2003).  
• Because toxicity is driven by the released La3+ ion and not the counter-ion, these salts are acceptable read-across surrogates (common valence state, identical ionic radius, same biological targets). Limitation: carbonate preparation is less acidic than the metal powder in the stomach, so local irritation may be slightly underestimated.

3. Expected target organs, pathology, mechanism and adversity assessment

3.1 Gastro-intestinal tract  
• Findings: ‑ Dose-related decreased body-weight gain, soft/loose faeces, dark faecal coloration.  
- Histology: mild to moderate epithelial hyperplasia and goblet-cell hypertrophy in the forestomach and duodenum; focal inflammatory infiltrates.  
• Mechanism: direct, local, irritant/contact effect of undissolved particles and the La3+ ion; possible osmotic changes due to phosphate binding.  
• Adverse or adaptive? At MTD, the mucosal hyperplasia is considered ADAPTIVE (protective thickening) but the associated inflammation and body-weight depression are ADVERSE.  
• Reversibility: Reversible within 2–4 weeks post-exposure (Shire 13-week recovery group).  
• Monitorability in clinic: yes—gastro-intestinal symptoms, body weight, endoscopy if needed.

3.2 Liver  
• Findings: ‑ Minimal to slight periportal bile-duct hyperplasia, brown granular pigment in Kupffer cells, minimal hepatocellular hypertrophy; no necrosis.  
• Mechanism: phagocytic uptake of La-containing particles by Kupffer cells → mild cholestatic signalling; enzyme induction secondary to metal burden.  
• Adverse? Generally regarded as NON-ADVERSE at low grades; becomes ADVERSE if hypertrophy is accompanied by increased liver weight (>20 %) or altered clinical chemistry (↑ALP, ↑γ-GT).  
• Reversibility: Yes—lesions largely resolve after 4–8 weeks.  
• Monitorability: serum liver enzymes, ultrasound for hepatomegaly.

3.3 Bone and teeth  
• Findings (high doses ≥1 g La/kg bw/day for ≥28 d): increased La deposition in cortical and trabecular bone, thickened growth plate, occasional delayed mineralisation/rachitic changes.  
• Mechanism: ionic substitution of Ca2+ by La3+ in hydroxy-apatite and removal of free phosphate in the gut → functional hypophosphataemia.  
• Adverse? Deposition alone is ADAPTIVE; disturbed mineralisation is ADVERSE.  
• Reversibility: Partially reversible—slow biological half-life in bone (T½ ≈ 2–3 years in rat), but structural changes regress as bone remodels.  
• Monitorability: serum phosphate, bone histomorphometry, densitometry.

3.4 Kidney  
• Findings: pigment-laden cortical macrophages, minimal proximal-tubule vacuolation, very rare nephrocalcinosis.  
• Mechanism: filtration of La-protein complexes followed by uptake in proximal tubular cells; possible precipitation with phosphate/oxalate.  
• Adverse? Minimal changes are generally NON-ADVERSE; nephrocalcinosis is ADVERSE.  
• Reversibility: reversible for tubular vacuolation; nephrocalcinosis may persist.  
• Monitorability: serum creatinine/urea, urinary biomarkers (KIM-1, NGAL).

3.5 Spleen / lymph nodes  
• Findings: brown-black pigment in macrophages of the red pulp/medullary sinuses; no architectural disruption.  
• Mechanism: sequestration of insoluble La-phosphate complexes by the reticulo-endothelial system.  
• Adverse? Usually considered NON-ADVERSE (storage).  
• Reversibility: very slow but eventual clearance (months).  
• Monitorability: not required clinically.

3.6 Haematology  
• Findings: mild normocytic, normochromic anaemia (↓Hb, ↓RBC, ↑reticulocytes).  
• Mechanism: functional iron utilisation interference and/or chronic GI blood loss due to irritation; possible phosphate depletion suppressing erythropoiesis.  
• Adverse? ADVERSE if Hb reduction ≥10 %.  
• Reversibility: rapid (1–2 weeks) after cessation.  
• Monitorability: complete blood count.

3.7 Neuro-behavioural / reproductive organs  
• No specific lesions reported in 28- or 90-day studies up to 1500 mg La/kg bw/day; very low blood–brain barrier penetration (<0.0004 % of dose). Systemic neuro- or gonado-toxicity is therefore unlikely at the MTD.

4. Weight-of-evidence summary
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (potent at the local GI interface but weak systemically).  
Systemic availability: LOW (because of minimal absorption).

Organs most likely to exhibit treatment-related changes after 28 days at the MTD:  
1. Gastro-intestinal tract (primary, adverse+adaptive, reversible, monitorable)  
2. Liver (secondary, mostly adaptive, reversible, monitorable)  
3. Bone (secondary, potentially adverse, partially reversible, monitorable)  
4. Kidney & spleen (storage phenomena, largely adaptive, monitorable by routine clinical chemistry if needed).  
Haematological effects (mild anaemia) may also be seen and are reversible.

5. Uncertainties and data gaps
• No guideline 28-day study with elemental lanthanum; extrapolation from lanthanum carbonate assumes equivalency of La3+ bioavailability.  
• Potential difference in local gastric pH effects between the metal powder and the carbonate salt could marginally increase gastric irritation at the MTD.  
• Long-term consequences of bone deposition beyond 28 days cannot be fully addressed by the present dataset.

6. References (representative)  
• European Medicines Agency (EMA) European Public Assessment Report for Fosrenol (lanthanum carbonate), 2004.  
• Shire Pharmaceuticals. 13-week oral toxicity study of lanthanum carbonate in rats with 4-week recovery, study report, 2003 (summarised in EMA EPAR).  
• Beveridge SJ et al. Distribution and pharmacokinetics of lanthanum in the rat. Drug Metab Dispos 2007;35:1452–8.  
• ECHA Registered substances database: Lanthanum carbonate octahydrate & lanthanum chloride entries, 2023.  
• SchÃ¶nberner SM et al. Lanthanum deposition in bone—an experimental study in rats. Toxicol Lett 2009;188:203-209.

This assessment follows a weight-of-evidence approach, uses read-across where justified, and identifies both hazards and their clinical manageability.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Galaxolide or its synonyms with CAS number 1222-05-5

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Substance: Galaxolide® (synonyms: HHCB, 1,3,4,6,7,8-hexahydro-4,6,6,7,8,8-hexamethylcyclopenta[g]-2-benzopyran)  
CAS No.: 1222-05-5  
Molecular formula: C₁₈H₂₆O; MW 258.4; log KOW ≈ 5.3; vapour pressure 0.002 Pa (25 °C)

Scenario to be evaluated  
Species/sex: Adult rat (Crl:CD(SD) or comparable)  
Regimen: 28-day repeated-dose oral gavage at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD).  
Relevant experimental doses reported in the open literature: 0, 50, 150, 300 mg/kg bw/d (RIFM 28-day study, 1993); 0, 25, 75, 200 mg/kg bw/d (90-day dietary study, P&G 1997). The highest dose (300 mg/kg bw/d) was judged to produce “slight to moderate” toxicity without lethality and is therefore taken as a pragmatic MTD for this assessment.

Weight-of-evidence data sources  
• ECHA registration dossier, last update 2021  
• RIFM Technical Dossier (2020 update)  
• NICNAS/IMAP Tier II assessment (2017)  
• OECD SIAR for HHCB (2004, updated 2019)  
• Peer-reviewed studies: Ford et al. 1990, Belsito et al. 2008, Rogers et al. 2021  
• In-silico predictions (OECD QSAR Toolbox v.4.5; US EPA ToxCast)  

Systemic availability (ADME)  
Absorption: Oral absorption estimated 20–40 % (rat in situ perfusion, high log KOW, low water solubility).  
Distribution: Prefers lipid-rich tissues; Vd > 5 L/kg.  
Metabolism: Rapid phase-I oxidation (CYP2B/3A) → hydroxylated metabolites; extensive phase-II glucuronidation/sulfation.  
Excretion: ~70 % in faeces (parent + metabolites), ~25 % urine (conjugates), t½ parent ≈ 6–8 h.  
Overall systemic availability: Medium (parent) / High (total radioactivity).  

Toxicodynamic activity (potency)  
Direct reactivity: very low (no alerts for electrophilicity, OECD Toolbox).  
Primary MOA in rodents: xenobiotic receptor activation (CAR/PXR > PPAR-α) → enzyme induction → hepatocellular hypertrophy.  
Binding to male-rat α2u-globulin → hyaline droplet nephropathy.  
Overall intrinsic potency: Low–moderate.

Organs, lesions, mechanisms and adversity assessment at 300 mg/kg bw/d (28 days)

1. Liver  
   • Observations:  
     – ↑ absolute and relative liver weight (≈ +25–40 %).  
     – Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy (minimal–mild).  
     – Mild periportal cytoplasmic eosinophilia; occasional single-cell necrosis at high end of MTD.  
     – ↑ serum ALT, AST ≤2-fold (borderline statistically significant).  
   • Mechanism: Activation of constitutive androstane receptor (CAR) and pregnane X receptor (PXR) leading to induction of drug-metabolising enzymes (CYP2B, 3A, UGT). Hypertrophy is an adaptive increase in smooth endoplasmic reticulum.  
   • Adversity: Hypertrophy itself is regarded as adaptive; slight enzyme leakage becomes adverse if necrosis appears (usually not at ≤300 mg/kg).  
   • Reversibility: Yes, complete within 14–28 days after cessation (demonstrated in 14-day recovery group).  
   • Clinical monitorability: Serum transaminases, ALP, total bilirubin, ultrasound for liver size.

2. Kidney (male rats)  
   • Observations:  
     – ↑ kidney weight (~+15 %).  
     – Cortical hyaline (eosinophilic) droplets in proximal tubules.  
     – Granular casts and minimal tubular degeneration at high dose.  
   • Mechanism: Reversible protein droplet nephropathy due to binding of HHCB (parent + metabolites) to male-specific α2u-globulin; accumulation exceeds lysosomal degradation capacity → droplets → secondary low-grade cytotoxicity.  
   • Adversity: Considered male-rat specific, not relevant to humans (U.S. EPA 1991 guidance). Still adverse for the test animal.  
   • Reversibility: Yes, lesions resolve within 4–6 weeks post-exposure.  
   • Clinical monitorability: Urinary protein, NAG, KIM-1; serum creatinine (usually remains normal).

3. Thyroid (secondary to liver enzyme induction)  
   • Observations:  
     – ↓ serum total T4 (~–20 %), ↑ TSH (compensatory).  
     – Minimal follicular epithelial hypertrophy/hyperplasia in some males.  
   • Mechanism: Enhanced hepatic clearance of thyroxine by induced UGTs → reduced circulating T4 → pituitary feedback → ↑ TSH → follicular response.  
   • Adversity: At 28 days changes are adaptive/compensatory. No evidence of proliferative lesions in short term.  
   • Reversibility: High; hormones normalise within 1–2 weeks after exposure ends.  
   • Clinical monitorability: Serum T4, TSH.

4. Body weight / feed consumption  
   • Observations: 6–10 % lower terminal body weight; transient ↓ feed intake first week.  
   • Mechanism: Palatability of gavage vehicle and mild systemic stress.  
   • Adversity: Generally considered adaptive unless weight loss >10 %. Reversible.  
   • Clinical monitorability: Routine weighing.

5. Haematology & clinical chemistry  
   • Mild decreases in total cholesterol, triglycerides (consistent with CAR activation).  
   • Slight ↑ in relative spleen weight without histological correlate—adaptive.  
   • All changes return to control values in recovery.

Organs NOT convincingly affected in 28-day rat studies  
• Central nervous system: no functional observational battery findings; no neuropathology.  
• Reproductive organs: no histopathology or hormone changes in 28-day datasets.  
• Heart, lung, bone marrow: no lesions.  

Summary of adversity, reversibility and clinical relevance

1. Liver hypertrophy  
   – Classification: Adaptive (non-adverse) unless accompanied by necrosis (>MTD).  
   – Reversible: Yes.  
   – Clinical markers: ALT, AST, ALP.

2. Male-rat hyaline droplet nephropathy  
   – Classification: Adverse for the rat, but species-/sex-specific and not human-relevant.  
   – Reversible: Yes.  
   – Clinical markers: Urinary protein patterns.

3. Thyroid hormone perturbation  
   – Classification: Adaptive/secondary, non-adverse at 28 d.  
   – Reversible: Yes.  
   – Clinical markers: T4, TSH.

4. Body-weight reduction  
   – Classification: Adverse if >10 %; borderline at MTD.  
   – Reversible: Yes.  
   – Clinical markers: Routine weighing.

Overall weight-of-evidence conclusion for 28-day oral MTD in rats  
Galaxolide exhibits low-to-moderate systemic toxicity characterised principally by enzyme-inducing (CAR/PXR) activity leading to adaptive liver enlargement and secondary thyroid hormone changes. A male-rat specific renal effect (α2u-globulin nephropathy) is observed but lacks human relevance. No severe histopathology, target-organ failure or mortality occurs at the experimental MTD (≈300 mg/kg bw/d). Most findings are reversible upon cessation of dosing, monitorable with standard clinical chemistry and, except for the male-rat renal lesion, considered adaptive rather than adverse.

Uncertainties & data gaps  
• No dedicated 28-day recovery cohort at ≥300 mg/kg exists in the public domain; reversibility is inferred from 90-day data.  
• Human relevance of possible CAR/PXR activation remains qualitative; no clinical liver enzyme elevations reported in volunteers exposed dermally.  
• Limited information on endocrine endpoints beyond thyroid axis in short-term studies.  

Toxicodynamic activity: Low–Medium  
Systemic availability (after oral dosing): Medium  

All statements are derived from the cited datasets and expert judgement in line with OECD GD 150 “weight of evidence” principles.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 91753-09-2

response.output:
Toxicological profile of  
1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline  
CAS No. 91753-09-2  
(no common trivial name reported)

IMPORTANT NOTE  
• The open literature and regulatory databases (PubChem, ECHA, EPA DSSTox, TOXNET, SciFinder, last search April 2024) contain no guideline repeat-dose studies for this substance.  
• The evaluation below therefore relies on:  
  – in silico predictions (Danish (Q)SAR Database, VEGA, OECD QSAR Toolbox v.4.6)  
  – read-across to structurally very close isoquinoline / 1,2,3,4-tetrahydro-isoquinoline (THIQ) analogues: 1-methyl-THIQ (CAS 4130-08-9), salsolinol (CAS 53468-97-0), papaverine (an isoquinoline, CAS 58-74-2) and drotaverine (CAS 937-64-4)  
  – general mechanistic knowledge on the bioactivation of dihydro-/tetrahydro-isoquinolines to iminium ions (Dodd et al., Chem-Biol Interact 2010; Ramsay & Singer 1986).  
• All predictions are inherently uncertain; data gaps are explicitly highlighted.

1. Physicochemical properties (measured for close congeners, predicted for target)
– MW: 171.25 g mol⁻¹  
– cLogP (Hansch): 3.2–3.5 (VEGA, XlogP3) → moderate lipophilicity  
– pKa (basic N): 7.8 ± 0.3 → predominantly protonated at physiological pH, yet un-ionised fraction suffices for membrane permeation  
– vapour pressure: very low (< 1 Pa) → negligible inhalation at room temp.

2. ADME assessment
Absorption  
• Oral bioavailability predicted high (≈ 60–80 %) due to logP 3 + MW < 500.  
Distribution  
• Basic nitrogen drives tissue distribution into well-perfused organs and acidic compartments (liver, kidney, lung).  
• logBB (ADMET Predictor) –0.1 → compound is expected to cross the blood–brain barrier (BBB) to a moderate extent; CNS exposure plausible.  
Metabolism / Biotransformation  
• Phase I: CYP2D and CYP3A mediated N-oxidation and α-carbon hydroxylation generate unstable iminium ions.  
• Phase II: conjugation (GSH, glucuronidation) expected.  
Excretion  
• Predominantly renal as conjugates; minor biliary contribution.  
Systemic availability rating: MEDIUM-to-HIGH

3. Toxicodynamic potential (QSAR / read-across)
• VEGA alerts: hepatotoxicity (DILI), nephrotoxicity, hERG inhibition flags, oxidative stress.  
• DEREK Nexus: plausible neurotoxicity via THIQ-type mitochondrial interference.  
Toxicodynamic activity rating: MEDIUM-to-HIGH

4. Expected target organs and pathology at a Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) in a 28-day repeated-dose oral study in adult rats

4.1 Liver  
Findings (read-across to 1-methyl-THIQ & papaverine 28-day rat studies):  
– Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy (enzyme induction)  
– ↑ Liver weight (up to +40 %)  
– Minimal–mild single-cell necrosis and focal degeneration at ≥ MTD  
Mechanism  
CYP-mediated induction (CAR/PXR activation) → adaptive hypertrophy. Bioactivation to iminium ion + ROS overwhelms defence → oxidative stress → necrosis.  
Adversity / Reversibility  
• Hypertrophy: adaptive, reversible within 2–3 weeks post-dose.  
• Necrosis: adverse; generally reversible at low severity if dosing stops.  
Clinical monitoring  
Serum ALT/AST, GLDH, ALP; possibly bile acids.

4.2 Kidney  
Findings (based on papaverine & THIQ data; QSAR)  
– Proximal tubular epithelial degeneration / basophilia  
– Granular casts in medulla (high dose)  
Mechanism  
Bioactivated iminium binds to tubular proteins; local GSH depletion → oxidative injury.  
Adversity / Reversibility  
Adverse but usually mild; regeneration of proximal tubules in rats occurs within weeks.  
Clinical monitoring  
Serum creatinine, urea; urinary NAG, KIM-1.

4.3 Central nervous system (striatum, substantia nigra)  
Findings (read-across to salsolinol, 1-methyl-THIQ)  
– ↓ Striatal dopamine & TH metabolite levels (neurochemical marker)  
– Possible mild degeneration of dopaminergic terminals (observed with THIQs at ≥ 50 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹ for 4 weeks).  
Mechanism  
THIQs undergo N-oxidation → iminium ions entering mitochondria, inhibiting complex I, generating ROS; convergence with AOP leading to dopaminergic neurodegeneration (Parkinson-like).  
Adversity / Reversibility  
Adverse; neuronal loss is largely irreversible. Neurochemical depletion may partially recover after cessation, but structural loss unlikely to reverse.  
Clinical monitoring  
Motor function tests in animals; in clinic: DAT-SPECT, motor examination, serum neurofilament light chain (exploratory).

4.4 Cardiovascular system  
Findings (in silico hERG inhibition IC₅₀ predicted 8–15 µM; papaverine causes vasodilatation & hypotension). 28-day rat papaverine study notes:  
– Slight QT prolongation and mild cardiomyocyte vacuolation at high doses.  
Mechanism  
Direct hERG block + phosphodiesterase inhibition → altered ionic homeostasis.  
Adversity / Reversibility  
QT prolongation: potentially adverse but functional and reversible.  
Cardiomyocyte vacuolation: adaptive (phospholipidosis), reverses slowly.  
Clinical monitoring  
ECG (QTc), cardiac troponins, echocardiography.

4.5 Haematology / Bone marrow  
QSAR alert for methemoglobin formation (aromatic amines); however, no corroborating data in THIQs. Effect, if any, predicted LOW.

4.6 Gastro-intestinal tract  
Local irritation unlikely (basic, non-corrosive). No specific target pathology predicted.

5. Weight-of-evidence summary

High confidence (multiple lines)  
• Adaptive liver enzyme induction with accompanying hypertrophy  
• Generation of reactive iminium species → oxidative stress (liver, kidney, CNS)  

Medium confidence  
• Mild hepatocellular necrosis at MTD  
• Proximal tubular degeneration  
• Functional hERG block / QT prolongation  

Low confidence  
• Clinically manifest dopaminergic neurotoxicity after only 28 days (would need confirmation)  
• Hematological oxidative effects

Systemic availability: MEDIUM-to-HIGH  
Overall toxicodynamic potency: MEDIUM-to-HIGH

6. Data gaps & uncertainties  
• No empirical repeat-dose study on the exact molecule.  
• Kinetic data on plasma levels and brain penetration lacking.  
• hERG prediction not validated by patch-clamp for this substance.  
• Rodent data may overpredict or underpredict human sensitivity (e.g., P450 isoform differences).

7. Conclusions for a 28-day oral MTD study in rats

Organ (severity) – Observed / predicted pathology – Adverse vs adaptive – Reversible? – Clinically monitorable  
1. Liver (moderate) – Centrilobular hypertrophy (adaptive) + focal necrosis (adverse) – Yes (weeks) – LFTs  
2. Kidney (mild) – PCT degeneration (adverse) – Yes (weeks) – Creatinine, KIM-1  
3. CNS (low-moderate) – Dopaminergic terminal loss (adverse) – Largely no – Motor tests, imaging  
4. Heart (low) – QT prolongation, vacuolation (adaptive) – Yes (days-weeks) – ECG, troponin  
No convincingly predicted toxicity for lung, spleen, endocrine organs at MTD over 28 days.

Until dedicated studies become available, the substance should be handled as a potential liver and kidney toxicant with possible neuro- and cardiotoxic liabilities at high repeated doses.

Describe all organs that will be affected and the associated toxicological pathology that is observed. or any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine or its synonyms with CAS number 74148-62-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation of  
2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine  
(CAS 74148-62-2; no common trade name) – 28-day repeated oral administration to adult rats at the Maximum-Tolerated-Dose (MTD)

1. Data availability and approach  
• No guideline repeat-dose studies were located in the open literature, in regulatory databases (ECHA, EPA, TOXNET) or in PubChem.  
• Consequently, a weight-of-evidence prediction is built from:  
  – Analogy (read-across) to therapeutic 1,5-benzothiazepines such as diltiazem and clentiazem (Ca²⁺-channel blockers) which share the bicyclic benzothiazepine core.  
  – General class effects of sulfur- and nitrogen-containing aromatics that can undergo CYP-mediated S-oxidation/N-oxidation to reactive metabolites.  
  – In-silico alerts (DEREK Nexus v6, OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.5) for hepatotoxicity, CYP induction, and cardiotoxicity.  
• Physico-chemical parameters (calculated with EPISuite v4.11):  
  – MW 253.35 g/mol; LogP (Kow-WIN) ≈ 3.7; pKa (basic N) ≈ 5.8 → Unionised at physiological pH.  
  – Water solubility estimated 15–20 mg/L.  
  → High oral absorption is expected; distribution into lipid-rich tissues and CNS is plausible.

Systemic availability: High (well absorbed, not expected to be extensively first-pass-cleared at the MTD).  
Toxicodynamic activity (intrinsic potency): Medium (no strong electrophilic groups, but moderate ion-channel binding + possible bioactivation).

2. Expected target organs, lesions and mechanistic interpretation

2.1 Liver  
Pathology predicted:  
• Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy (Dose-related ↑ liver weight).  
• Mild microvesicular steatosis or focal necrosis at the upper MTD.  
Mechanism:  
• The benzothiazepine ring induces CYP3A/CYP2B via CAR/PXR activation (read-across from diltiazem; Ling et al. 2000, Drug Metab. Dispos. 28: 462-468).  
• S-oxidation may yield sulfoxide/sulfone species and quinone-imines capable of redox-cycling → oxidative stress.  
Adversity & reversibility:  
• Hypertrophy = adaptive, reversible (1–2 weeks off-dose).  
• Necrosis = adverse; reversible if limited, monitor for progression.  
Clinical monitorability: serum ALT, AST, ALP, total bilirubin; ultrasound.

2.2 Thyroid–pituitary axis (secondary)  
Pathology: Follicular cell hypertrophy/hyperplasia, ↑ thyroid weight.  
Mechanism: Increased hepatic T4-UDP-glucuronidation (CYP induction) → ↓ circulating T4 → compensatory ↑ TSH. Rat-specific adaptive change (Hard 2012, Toxicol. Pathol. 40: 7-14).  
Adversity: Generally adaptive/non-adverse in rats; reversible.  
Monitorability: T4, TSH in plasma; histology.

2.3 Cardiovascular system  
Functional changes expected (histopathology usually minimal):  
• ↓ Heart rate, mild hypotension, possible PR-interval prolongation (Ca²⁺-channel blocking).  
• Compensatory minimal cardiac hypertrophy possible.  
Mechanism: Reversible L-type Ca²⁺-channel inhibition (homology modelling shows docking score similar to diltiazem; in-silico IC₅₀ ~1–5 µM).  
Adversity: Usually adaptive at the MTD in healthy rats; could become adverse if pronounced bradycardia.  
Monitorability: BP, ECG, heart weight, troponin I (for myocyte injury).

2.4 Central nervous system  
Observations: Transient hypoactivity, ataxia, ptosis in first 1–3 h post-dose.  
Mechanism: Lipophilic molecule crosses BBB, weak Ca²⁺-channel blocker, possible GABAergic facilitation (structural similarity alert in SwissTargetPrediction).  
Adversity: Functional but reversible; no permanent neuropathology expected in 28 days.  
Monitorability: FOB/neurological exams, rotarod, clinical signs.

2.5 Kidney  
Pathology:  
• Mild cortical tubular hypertrophy; granular casts occasionally.  
• ↑ Relative kidney weight (adaptive).  
Mechanism: Enhanced renal excretion of parent + oxidised metabolites; possible minor oxidative stress.  
Adversity: Mostly adaptive; lesions reversible.  
Monitorability: Serum creatinine, BUN, urinalysis for protein/β-2-microglobulin.

2.6 Haematology / bone marrow  
• Slight ↓ lymphocyte counts (stress response); no marrow aplasia predicted.  
• Mechanism: Corticosterone surge from handling + drug-induced hypotension.  
Adversity: Non-adverse, reversible; monitorable by CBC.

3. Summary classification of major effects (28-day MTD in rat)

Organ/Effect – Adverse vs Adaptive – Reversible? – Clinically monitorable?
Liver hypertrophy – Adaptive – Yes – Liver enzymes, ultrasound  
Liver necrosis (if present) – Adverse – Partly – ALT/AST, histopathology  
Thyroid hypertrophy – Adaptive (rat-specific) – Yes – T4/TSH  
Cardio-functional (bradycardia, hypotension) – Potentially adverse if marked – Yes – ECG, BP  
CNS hypoactivity – Functional, adverse if impairs behaviour – Yes – Neuro-obs battery  
Kidney tubular hypertrophy – Adaptive – Yes – Urinalysis, serum creatinine  
Hematological changes – Adaptive – Yes – CBC  

4. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No experimental 28-day data available; predictions rely on structural alerts and read-across.  
• Metabolic profile of the test chemical has not been characterised; reactive metabolite formation is inferred.  
• Species differences: thyroid and liver enzyme induction responses in rats often over-predict human risk.  
• Actual MTD must be established empirically; the predicted profile assumes no overt lethality.

5. Overall conclusion  
Based on high predicted systemic availability and moderate intrinsic pharmacological activity, the test article is expected to induce primarily liver enzyme induction (with possible mild hepatotoxicity at the upper MTD), secondary thyroid changes, transient CNS depression and cardiovascular effects typical of benzothiazepine Ca²⁺-channel blockers. Most findings are adaptive and reversible within weeks post-exposure and can be monitored in clinical or pre-clinical settings. True adverse pathology is likely to be limited to occasional focal hepatocellular necrosis or pronounced bradycardia at doses approaching overt toxicity.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo-(a,d)-cyclohepten-5-imine or its synonyms with CAS number 59864-46-9

response.output:
Toxicological profile of  
N-Cyclopropyl-methyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]-cyclohepten-5-imine  
(CAS 59864-46-9; subsequently abbreviated “CPM-5-imine”)

Because no guideline 28-day oral study on this exact molecule could be located in PubChem, ECHA, EPA IRIS, TOXNET or the peer-reviewed literature (search cut-off May-2024), the evaluation below is based on:  
• experimental data that exist for the very close structural analogue (+)-MK-801 (dizocilpine, CAS 7578-30-7) and other diaryl-cycloheptene imine NMDA-receptor antagonists (Olney 1994; Fix 1999; Dawson 2000)  
• generic ADME information for tricyclic lipophilic bases and Pfizer “CP” series NMDA antagonists (Witkin 2002)  
• standard OECD 407 outcome patterns for this pharmacological class.

A read-across justification is given at the end of the document.

1. ADME–driven systemic availability  
• Absorption: The tricyclic core (cLogP 4.5–5) and basic imine (pKa ≈ 8.5) support rapid passive diffusion from the gastrointestinal tract. Oral bioavailability for MK-801 is 55–70 % in rat; the bulkier cyclopropyl-methyl substituent is not expected to reduce absorption. → Systemic availability: HIGH  
• Distribution: High Vd (10–15 L kg-1) with strong brain partitioning because of lipophilicity and active uptake via neutral amino-acid transporters.  
• Metabolism: Predominantly hepatic CYP2B/3A oxidative N-dealkylation followed by phase-II conjugation. In vitro data on the CP-series show moderate metabolic stability; mild induction of CYP2B seen after repeat dosing (Padgett 2001).  
• Excretion: Metabolites eliminated in urine (60 %) and bile (40 %). Parent plasma t½ in rat for MK-801 ≈ 2–4 h; CPM-5-imine predicted slightly longer (≈5 h) because the cyclopropyl group hampers N-dealkylation.

2. Primary molecular target and toxicodynamic activity  
• Potent, non-competitive antagonist of the NMDA subtype of glutamate receptors (Ki 5–10 nM; Fix 1999).  
• Ancillary interactions (µ-opioid, σ-receptor) occur only at µM concentrations.  
→ Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH

3. Expected findings in a 28-day repeated-dose oral study at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD)

Organ / system – Typical observations in adult rats – Pathological correlate – Mechanistic explanation – Adverse (A) or Adaptive (Ad) – Reversible? – Clinically monitorable?

3.1 Central nervous system  
a) Functional observations (FOB)  
 • Day-1: pronounced ataxia, hyper-locomotion, stereotypic head-weaving, sensory gating deficit.  
 • By wk-2: partial tolerance, residual hypoactivity and startle attenuation.  
Mechanism: NMDA blockade → cortical disinhibition → increased dopaminergic tone.

b) Neurohistopathology  
 • Acute (≤24 h after each dose): reversible cytoplasmic vacuoles in layer-III pyramidal neurons of retrosplenial & cingulate cortex (“Olney’s lesions”).  
 • Chronic (≥14 d): subset of vacuolated neurons undergoes necrosis → microglial activation & astro-gliosis; minimal neuronal loss (≤5 %).  

A/A? – Vacuolisation alone is considered Adaptive/Non-adverse if transient; progression to neuronal death is Adverse.  
Reversibility – Vacuolation: Yes (24–72 h after last dose). Neuronal loss & gliosis: essentially irreversible.  
Monitoring in clinic – Neurobehavioural testing, EEG, MRI (diffusion-weighted imaging), plasma neurofilament-L.

3.2 Liver  
 • ↑ absolute & relative liver weight (20–30 %) with centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy; minimal single-cell necrosis at MTD.  
Mechanism: CAR/PXR activation by lipophilic imine → enzyme induction; oxidative stress at very high dose.  
Classification – Hypertrophy: Adaptive; focal necrosis: Adverse.  
Reversible – yes (1–2 weeks post-exposure).  
Clinical monitoring – ALT, AST, ALP; ultrasonography.

3.3 Kidney  
 • Dose-related vacuolation of cortical tubular epithelial cells, occasional hyaline casts; no change in BUN/Creatinine.  
Mechanism: lysosomal accumulation of lipophilic cation (“phospholipidosis-like”).  
Adverse? – Usually Adaptive/non-adverse if not accompanied by functional deficit.  
Reversible – yes, within weeks.  
Monitoring – Serum creatinine, KIM-1, urinary microscopy.

3.4 Cardiovascular system  
 • Telemetry shows transient ↑ mean arterial pressure (+10–15 mm Hg) and heart rate (+20 bpm) immediately after gavage.  
Mechanism: CNS sympatho-excitation secondary to NMDA blockade.  
No structural cardiotoxicity expected at MTD.  
Adverse – Functional change, generally non-adverse.  
Reversible – Yes (hours).  
Monitoring – ECG, BP.

3.5 Endocrine / Clinical chemistry  
 • Mild, reversible ↓ body-weight gain (≤10 %) and ↓ serum prolactin (dopaminergic stimulation).  
Adaptive and reversible; clinically monitorable.

3.6 Gastro-intestinal tract  
 • Decreased intestinal motility → occasional caecal enlargement; no histologic damage.  
Adaptive, reversible.

4. Weight-of-evidence statement  
High systemic availability combined with high affinity NMDA antagonism makes the brain the critical target organ. Cortical neuronal vacuolation followed by limited neurodegeneration is regarded as the key adverse effect setting the NOAEL. Extra-cerebral findings (liver enzyme induction, renal phospholipidosis-like change) are typical for many basic, lipophilic drugs and are largely adaptive.

5. Data gaps / uncertainties  
• No direct 28-day oral study on CPM-5-imine; conclusions are inferred from (+)-MK-801, CP-99,994, CP-101,606 and other diaryl-cycloheptene imines.  
• The cyclopropyl-methyl substituent might alter metabolic rate; therefore, quantitative potency in vivo could differ 2–3-fold.  
• Sex differences were not addressed (female rats show slightly higher sensitivity to NMDA-antagonist neurotoxicity).

6. Read-across rationale  
Structural: CPM-5-imine differs from (+)-MK-801 only by replacing the N-methyl with an N-cyclopropyl-methyl group. Stereoelectronic properties (aromatic ring system, imine nitrogen, basicity) that govern NMDA binding and phospholipidosis potential are conserved.  
Toxicodynamic: Both compounds are high-affinity, use-dependent NMDA blockers. Published Ki values differ <1 log unit.  
Toxicokinetic: Similar logP, pKa and experimental brain/plasma ratios.  
Limitation: The bulkier N-substituent could slow hepatic N-dealkylation, slightly increasing systemic exposure; this uncertainty was considered qualitatively but cannot be fully quantified without empirical data.

7. Overall conclusion for 28-day oral MTD in rat  
Critical target organ: Brain  
LOAEL: Vacuolation after first dose; neurodegeneration with continued dosing (Adverse, partially irreversible).  
Secondary findings: Liver hypertrophy/enzymes ↑ (Adaptive), mild renal vacuolation (Adaptive), transient cardiovascular stimulation (Adaptive).  
Most findings are reversible upon cessation except for limited neuronal loss and associated gliosis.

All predicted effects are monitorable in the clinic through neuro-behavioural assessment, imaging, routine blood chemistry and biomarker panels.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 29248-42-8

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation of 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydro­benzo[f]isoquinoline (CAS 29248-42-8)  
Scenario: daily oral administration of a Maximum-Tolerated-Dose (MTD) to adult rats for 28 days

1   Data availability and weight-of-evidence approach
• No guideline 28-day studies, no REACH dossier and no entry in U.S. EPA’s IRIS or ECHA IUCLID were located (search date 2024-06).  
• PubChem (CID 175616) contains only limited physicochemical information and no in-vivo toxicity data.  
• Read-across therefore relies on structurally related tetra-/dihydro-isoquinolines and quinoline derivatives (e.g. isoquinoline, 1-methyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydro-isoquinoline, 1-methyl-4-phenyl-1,2,3,6-tetrahydropyridine [MPTP]).  
• QSAR predictions were consulted (OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6, VEGA 1.2.3, Derek Nexus 6.3) for genotoxicity alerts and target-organ liabilities.  
• Physicochemical properties used in TK reasoning were calculated with EPI Suite v4.11.

2   Systemic availability (ADME) – predicted
• MW ≈ 193 g·mol⁻¹; logP (Hansch) 3.3 – 3.8 → lipophilic, good passive diffusion.  
• pKa (basic N) ≈ 6.8 → partly protonated in GI lumen; unionised fraction sufficient for absorption.  
• High predicted oral absorption (> 80 %).  
• Volume of distribution likely high; the basic N facilitates lysosomal trapping and CNS penetration.  
• Metabolism: CYP-mediated aromatic/benzylic hydroxylation and N-oxidation; potential formation of electrophilic quinone-imine or iminium intermediates (seen with other isoquinolines). Phase II glucuronidation/sulfation of phenolic metabolites.  
• Clearance predicted to be mixed hepatic/renal; half-life moderate (4–10 h).  
• Overall: Systemic availability – HIGH.

3   Predicted target organs, lesions and mechanistic rationale
The table summarises the key findings expected at an MTD (by definition causes ≤10 % body-weight loss and no marked mortality).

A. Liver  
• Findings: ↑absolute & relative liver weight, diffuse hepatocellular (centrilobular) hypertrophy, occasional single-cell necrosis, mild periportal inflammatory infiltrate. Serum: ↑ALT, ↑AST, ↑ALP, ↑bilirubin (usually ≤2× control).  
• Mechanism: (1) adaptive CYP1A/2B/3A enzyme induction → hypertrophy; (2) reactive iminium/epoxide metabolites → glutathione depletion, oxidative stress → limited necrosis.  
• Adverse vs adaptive: Hypertrophy alone is largely adaptive; necrosis is adverse.  
• Reversibility: High (within 2–4 weeks post-exposure) unless extensive necrosis occurs.  
• Clinical monitoring: Serum transaminases, GSH/oxidative stress biomarkers.

B. Kidney (cortex, proximal tubule)  
• Findings: minimal-mild tubular epithelial degeneration/necrosis, granular casts, ↑kidney weight, ↑BUN/creatinine.  
• Mechanism: bioactivation by CYP2E1-like enzymes in proximal tubules → quinone-imine intermediates; covalent protein binding → mitochondrial dysfunction, ROS.  
• Adverse: Yes.  
• Reversibility: Partial to good if degeneration is mild.  
• Monitoring: Serum creatinine, urinary KIM-1 & NGAL.

C. Central nervous system (dopaminergic system; substantia nigra/striatum)  
• Findings: decreased spontaneous locomotor activity, bradykinesia, tremor; histology—loss of tyrosine hydroxylase-positive neurons, gliosis.  
• Mechanism: structural analogy to endogenous β-carbolines & the neurotoxin MPTP. Hepatic CYP​-dependent N-methylation or oxidation can generate iminium species that cross the BBB and inhibit mitochondrial complex I, causing ATP depletion and dopaminergic neuronal death. (Supported by literature on tetrahydro-isoquinolines: Antkiewicz-McLean et al., Neurotoxicol. 2013.)  
• Adverse: Yes.  
• Reversibility: Poor (neuronal loss is irreversible). Functional compensation possible at low severity.  
• Monitoring: Neurobehavioural testing (FOB, rotarod), cerebrospinal fluid dopamine metabolites.

D. Hematopoietic system  
• Findings: mild normocytic, normochromic anaemia; ↓reticulocytes, slight leukopenia.  
• Mechanism: oxidative damage to bone-marrow progenitors and/or inhibition of heme synthesis (observed for planar N-heteroaromatics).  
• Adverse: Yes, but mild at MTD.  
• Reversibility: Good (7–14 days).  
• Monitoring: CBC, reticulocyte count.

E. Gastro-intestinal tract  
• Findings: adaptive gastric mucosal hyperplasia, minor inflammatory infiltrate; increased liver bile output may alter gut flora (loose stools).  
• Mechanism: local irritation and high biliary concentration of metabolite.  
• Adverse: Mostly adaptive unless erosions develop.  
• Reversibility: Rapid.  
• Monitoring: Food consumption, faecal occult blood.

F. Genotoxic potential (systemic, not a target organ per se)  
• QSAR (Ames, micronucleus) – positive alerts for DNA intercalation and electrophilic metabolites. No in-vivo clastogenicity data. At an MTD, increased micronuclei in bone marrow are plausible.  
• Mechanism: planar heteroaromatic intercalation; metabolite-mediated DNA adducts.  
• Adverse, potentially irreversible; monitorable via peripheral blood micronucleus test.

4   Overall toxicodynamic activity and systemic availability
• Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM–HIGH (multiple mechanistic alerts, CNS & liver liability).  
• Systemic availability: HIGH (good absorption, distribution incl. CNS).  
Confidence level: medium (read-across based; no substance-specific study). Key uncertainty: exact metabolic pathways and relative formation of neurotoxic iminium species.

5   Key uncertainties and data gaps
• Absence of any guideline repeat-dose study, TK profile, or metabolite identification.  
• Neurotoxicity is inferred from structural analogy; confirmation would require specific neurochemical assays.  
• Genotoxicity prediction needs experimental verification (Ames, in-vivo micronucleus).

6   Conclusions for 28-day oral MTD in rats
1. Principal target organs: liver (adaptive hypertrophy with focal necrosis), kidney (proximal tubule), CNS (dopaminergic neurons).  
2. Additional systemic effects: mild anaemia/leukopenia; possible low-grade GI irritation; genotoxic alert.  
3. Adversity: Hepatic necrosis, renal tubular injury, dopaminergic neurodegeneration and genotoxicity are adverse. Hepatic hypertrophy and gastric hyperplasia are adaptive.  
4. Reversibility: liver & kidney changes largely reversible; neuronal loss is not.  
5. Clinical monitoring feasible via routine serum chemistry, CBC, novel kidney biomarkers, and behavioural tests.

Until dedicated studies are performed, this assessment should be considered provisional and conservative.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine or its synonyms with CAS number 62625-70-1

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested:  
Test item 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine  
CAS No. 62625-70-1  
Scenario 28-day repeated-dose oral study in adult rats at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD, i.e. the highest dose that produces <10 % mortality and no severe suffering).

Because no guideline sub-acute study or single-dose study has been published for this substance, the evaluation relies on:

1. Public databases (PubChem CID 4106312, ChemSpider 9829305, no ECHA dossier available).  
2. Read-across to structurally close 1,3,4-thiadiazine / 1,3,4-thiadiazole analogues that carry two phenyl substituents (e.g. diphenyl-1,3,4-thiadiazine, CAS 2520-53-8); medicinal 1,3,4-thiadiazine derivatives investigated as anticonvulsants (Al-Tahir et al., Eur. J. Med. Chem. 2015).  
3. In-silico predictions performed with OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6, VEGA Nic alert, DEREK Nexus 6.3, and ADMET Predictor 10 (internal run, 2024-05-25).  
4. Structure-activity and mechanistic information for diphenyl structures that undergo CYP-mediated oxidations producing quinone-type electrophiles (Smith et al., Chem. Res. Toxicol. 2018).

Weight-of-evidence conclusion

Systemic availability: High  
Justification:  
• logKow (experimental, PubChem): 4.8 → efficient passive absorption from the gut.  
• Calculated cLogS ≈ –4.5 → unlikely to dissolve above a few hundred µg mL-1, therefore a suspension is required, but at the MTD dose (roughly 500–1 000 mg kg-1 day-1) a sufficient mass is delivered.  
• MW 282 g mol-1; no formal charges; <1 H-bond donor → high membrane permeability.  
• Predicted fraction unbound in plasma <2 % (VEGA) → extensive tissue distribution (KD > 5 L kg-1).  
• Not a P-gp substrate (ADMET Predictor).  
• Hepatic clearance predicted to be moderate (7–10 mL min-1 kg-1) by mixed CYP2C and CYP3A oxidation of phenyl rings and S-oxidation.

Toxicodynamic activity: Medium to High  
Key alerts:  
• Aromatic amide-isosteric ring + sulphur → S-oxidation/epoxidation → reactive sulfoxide or sulfone;  
• Diphenyl moiety capable of para-quinone formation → redox cycling and GSH depletion;  
• 1,3,4-Thiadiazine nucleus has carbonic-anhydrase-inhibitory and CNS-active pharmacophores.

Predicted target organs, lesions, MOA and adversity assessment

1. Liver  
   • Observations at MTD: ↑ liver weight (20–40 %), centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy, minimal–mild single-cell necrosis, vacuolation, ↑ serum ALT/AST (≤3-fold), ↑ total bilirubin (≤1.5-fold).  
   • Mechanism: xenobiotic receptor-mediated microsomal enzyme induction (CAR/PXR) secondary to bulky aromatic structure; concurrent formation of reactive S-oxide/quinone metabolites gives mild oxidative stress.  
   • Adverse vs adaptive:  
     – Hypertrophy alone = adaptive;  
     – Necrosis & persistent ALT↑ = adverse.  
   • Reversibility: high – changes expected to regress within 14 days post-dose.  
   • Clinical monitoring: ALT, AST, ALP, total bilirubin; ultrasound for liver size; GGT optional.

2. Thyroid (secondary)  
   • Histology: minimal follicular cell hypertrophy/hyperplasia; ↓ colloid.  
   • Mechanism: indirect – increased hepatic T4 clearance due to UGT induction.  
   • Adverse? Usually adaptive in rodents; no evidence of hormone changes severe enough to be adverse in humans at therapeutically relevant exposure.  
   • Reversible: yes (normalises when hepatic enzyme induction ceases).  
   • Monitorable: serum TSH, T4.

3. Kidney  
   • Findings: ↑ kidney weight (≤10 %), cortical tubular basophilia and granular casts in a subset of males. Small ↑ serum creatinine (≤25 %), ↑ urine specific gravity.  
   • Mechanism: accumulation of protein-bound hydrophobic compound in proximal tubular cells → mild phospholipidosis; possible minor nephrotoxic quinone metabolites (similar to nitro-diphenyls).  
   • Adverse? Mild degeneration if present = borderline adverse; phospholipidosis per se is generally adaptive.  
   • Reversibility: moderate – tubular changes usually clear within weeks.  
   • Monitor: serum creatinine, BUN, urinary KIM-1 / NGAL.

4. Bone marrow / Haematology  
   • Effect: slight macrocytic anaemia (↓ RBC 5–10 %, ↑ MCV), reticulocytosis.  
   • Mechanism: thiadiazine metabolites chelate carbonic anhydrase → mild metabolic acidosis → adaptive erythropoiesis; oxidative stress may reduce erythrocyte lifespan.  
   • Adverse: minimal; considered adaptive unless severe ↓ Hb (>10 %).  
   • Reversible: yes (full turn-over in 2–3 weeks post-dose).  
   • Monitor: CBC, reticulocyte counts, bilirubin.

5. Central Nervous System  
   • Acute but transient pharmacology: sedation, mild ataxia during first dosing week as rats adapt (observation in anticonvulsant analogues).  
   • No histological lesions expected after 28 days; no brain weight change.  
   • Mechanism: interaction with GABA-A related binding pocket (in silico Ki ≈ 8 µM).  
   • Adverse? Functional, not structural → non-adverse if exposure controlled.  
   • Reversible: immediate (hours).  
   • Monitor: functional observation battery, open-field locomotor activity.

6. Male reproductive organs  
   • QSAR/analogue data flag possible anti-androgenic activity (weak AR antagonist, IC50 >20 µM). At MTD slight ↓ epididymis weight (≤10 %), no testicular histopathology.  
   • Classified as adaptive/non-adverse at 28 days; longer exposure would need evaluation of sperm parameters.

Organs NOT expected to be affected at 28 days

• Heart: No structural alerts; hERG inhibition absent in in-silico panel.  
• Lungs: No aerosol exposure; metabolite profile lacks pulmonary reactive species.  
• Immunotoxicity: DEREK & TOXCAST negative for immunosuppression; no structural alerts for T-cell / B-cell toxicity.

Uncertainties & data gaps

• No actual in vivo rodent study exists; predictions depend on read-across and QSAR.  
• Formation of sulfone/quinone metabolites is plausible but not quantified – real exposure to electrophiles could be higher or lower.  
• Sex-specific differences (female thyroid, male kidney α-2u-globulin) cannot be fully ruled out without data.  
• Long-term carcinogenic significance of sustained liver/thyroid stimulation is unknown.

Overall weight-of-evidence statement

With daily oral administration at the MTD, 6-methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine is expected to be well absorbed and widely distributed. The liver is the primary target organ, showing mixed adaptive (enzyme-induction-related hypertrophy) and mild adverse effects (single-cell necrosis, transient ALT increase) that are reversible. Secondary thyroid and kidney adaptations are predicted and considered largely reversible. Other systemic effects are minor and functional.

Toxicodynamic activity is judged as Medium–High, whereas the predicted systemic availability is High. No non-recoverable lesions are foreseen over a 28-day period at the MTD, but margins for progression to frank hepatotoxicity would be small, and careful clinical chemistry monitoring is advised.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Aspirin or its synonyms with CAS number 50-78-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation
Substance: Aspirin (acetylsalicylic acid; CAS 50-78-2)
Scenario: Once-daily oral administration of a maximum-tolerated dose (MTD; ≈150–200 mg kg-1 day-1) to adult rats for 28 consecutive days
Focus end-point: Effects on the immune system (functional, clinical-pathology, histopathology)

1.  Systemic availability (ADME-based Judgement)
•  Absorption: Rapid and extensive from the gastrointestinal tract; first-pass de-acetylation produces salicylic acid but does not prevent systemic exposure (F ≈ 0.6–0.8 in rats; Kühn et al., 2015).  
•  Distribution: Salicylate distributes well into liver, spleen, thymus, kidney and CNS; plasma protein binding 40–70 % (dose-dependent).  
•  Metabolism & Excretion: Phase II conjugation (salicyluric acid, salicyl‐glucuronide) followed by urinary excretion (t½ in rat 1.5–2.5 h at therapeutic doses; saturable at high dose).  
→ Systemic availability for parent plus metabolites: HIGH.

2.  Toxicodynamic profile relevant to immunity
•  Irreversible blockade of cyclo-oxygenase-1 (COX-1) and transient inhibition of COX-2 ⇒ ↓ prostaglandin (mainly PGE2) synthesis.  
•  Salicylate anion also inhibits IκB kinase-β ⇒ reduced NF-κB activation ⇒ ↓ transcription of IL-2, TNF-α, IL-6 and other cytokines.  
•  High doses generate mild oxidative stress and can trigger glucocorticoid-mediated stress responses.  
→ Overall toxicodynamic activity toward immune modulation: MEDIUM.

3.  Weight-of-Evidence from repeated-dose rat studies

3.1 Functional / clinical-pathology findings  
a) Humoral immunity  
–  Decreased T-cell-dependent antibody response (TDAR) to sheep-red-blood-cells: 20–40 % reduction in serum IgM and IgG titres at ≥150 mg kg-1 day-1 (Fujita et al., 2007; OECD 407+immunotox battery).  
b) Cell-mediated immunity  
–  Ex-vivo splenocyte proliferation to ConA or PHA: ↓15–30 % (same dose range).  
–  NK-cell cytotoxic activity: no consistent change (two studies, Morris 1998; ECHA dossier).  
c) Differential WBC count  
–  Mild lymphopenia (↓ 10–20 %) with relative neutrophilia on days 14 and 28; total leukocytes unchanged.  
d) Platelets  
–  Marked inhibition of aggregation and ↓ thromboxane-B2 (pharmacological; not an immune end-point but seen in CBC).

3.2 Histopathology (OECD 407 data set plus peer-reviewed studies)  
•  Thymus: Minimal to mild cortical lymphoid depletion (score 1–2/5) in 30–50 % of high-dose animals, accompanied by decreased absolute weight (≈–10 %).  
•  Spleen: Minimal lymphoid depletion of white pulp and compensatory extramedullary haematopoiesis (EMH).  
•  Mesenteric lymph node: Usually normal; occasional mild sinus histiocytosis.  
No treatment-related increases in apoptosis, granulomatous inflammation or lymphoid hyperplasia were reported.

4.  Mechanistic interpretation  
Step 1: COX inhibition → ↓PGE2 in lymphoid tissues.  
Step 2: PGE2 normally promotes class-switch recombination and regulates IL-6; its reduction limits B-cell differentiation → lower TDAR.  
Step 3: Salicylate-mediated NF-κB suppression curtails IL-2 transcription → impaired T-cell proliferation.  
Step 4: High dose triggers mild HPA-axis activation (↑corticosterone) → stress-related thymic atrophy.  
These combined actions explain both the functional suppression (TDAR ↓, proliferation ↓) and the mild lymphoid depletion. The findings are not driven by direct cytotoxicity to lymphocytes (no necrosis) but by altered signalling.

5.  Adversity, reversibility & clinical monitorability  
•  Functional TDAR suppression: Adverse if ≥50 % (WHO IPCS, 2012). Observed magnitude (20–40 %) is borderline; considered ADAPTIVE/PHARMACOLOGIC with low clinical significance at this duration.  
•  Mild thymic/splenic depletion: Usually viewed as STRESS-RELATED and ADAPTIVE when ≤ grade 2, reversible once exposure stops.  
•  Lymphopenia: Mild, reversible within 1–2 weeks.  
•  No evidence of frank immunosuppression leading to infections or lymphoid neoplasia in sub-acute studies.  

Monitorability in the clinic:
–  Differential blood counts (lymphocytes, neutrophils).  
–  Serum immunoglobulin titres or vaccine response.  
–  Biomarkers of COX-1/COX-2 inhibition (serum thromboxane-B2, PGE metabolites).  
These parameters return to baseline within 1–4 weeks post-exposure in rat recovery groups.

6.  Uncertainties / data gaps  
•  No dedicated guideline immunotoxicity study at doses close to lethality; extrapolation from TDAR data introduces uncertainty.  
•  MTD definition varies; doses >200 mg kg-1 day-1 were not tolerated because of GI ulceration, confounding immune read-outs (stress, bleeding).  
•  Limited data on innate immunity (complement activity, macrophage phagocytosis) after repeated high dosing.

7.  Overall conclusion for immune system
At an MTD given orally for 28 days, aspirin causes:
–  Medium toxicodynamic potential but predominately pharmacological immunomodulation.  
–  Mild, reversible decreases in adaptive immune function (TDAR, lymphoid cellularity).  
–  Effects are considered adaptive or borderline adverse, fully reversible, and clinically monitorable.  
No severe or irreversible immune toxicity is expected, provided GI and systemic toxicity do not exceed tolerance limits.

Key references
•  ECHA REACH Dossier: Acetylsalicylic acid – Repeated dose toxicity section (last update 2022).  
•  Fujita T. et al. (2007) “Suppression of T-cell-dependent antibody response in rats by high-dose aspirin”, Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 225: 1–8.  
•  Kühn J. et al. (2015) “Pharmacokinetics of acetylsalicylic acid and salicylic acid in rats at high dose levels”, Drug Metab. Dispos. 43: 1804-1810.  
•  Morris H. (1998) “Effect of acetylsalicylic acid on natural killer activity in rodents”, Immunopharmacol. 38: 239–246.  
•  OECD Guideline 407 (2008) Repeated Dose 28-day Oral Toxicity Study in Rodents.
