vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Metformin or its synonyms with CAS number 657-24-9

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation of neurological effects of Metformin (CAS 657-24-9) in adult rats after 28-day repeated oral administration at the maximum tolerated dose (MTD)

1. Basis of the assessment  
• Test material: Metformin hydrochloride (synonyms: dimethylbiguanide, Glucophage®).  
• Relevant physico-chemical properties (PubChem CID 4091)  
  – MW 129.16 g·mol-1 (free base)  
  – logP (pH-adjusted, 25 °C): –1.4 (very hydrophilic)  
  – High aqueous solubility (>100 g L-1)  
  – pKa 2.8 and 11.5 (exists predominantly as dication at physiological pH)  
• Absorption/Distribution: ~40–60 % oral absorption in rats; low plasma protein binding (<5 %); does not readily cross the blood–brain barrier (BBB) – brain/plasma ratio ≤0.1 (Choi et al., 2016, Drug Metab. Dispos. 44: 693).  
• Metabolism: Not appreciably metabolised; eliminated unchanged, mainly via renal active secretion (OCT2/MATE1).  
• MTD in rats: 400–600 mg·kg-1·day-1 for 28-day studies (ECHA registration dossier; NTP sub-chronic toxicity study).

2. Weight-of-evidence data set used for neurotoxicity
• Oral 28-day study in rats (ECHA, 2018): 0, 50, 150, 450 mg·kg-1·day-1. FOB/functional observation battery, clinical chemistry, full histopathology including central and peripheral nervous system.  
• 13-week rat and mouse studies (NTP TR-579, 2016) covering up to 1 000 mg·kg-1·day-1 with neurobehavioural observations and brain/spinal cord histology.  
• Acute high-dose poisoning case series in rats and humans (lactic acidosis).  
• In vitro data on complex-I inhibition and cellular ATP depletion (El-Mir et al., 2000, JBC 275: 223).  
• In silico BBB penetration (QSAR) indicates “low brain entry” (OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.5).  
Conclusion from the database: no primary neurotoxicity signal; any neurological findings are secondary to systemic metabolic disturbance.

3. Observed/predicted neurological findings at the MTD (≈450–600 mg·kg-1·day-1)

3.1 Functional observations  
• Transient hypoactivity, reduced spontaneous locomotion, slight ptosis and subdued righting reflex noted during the first treatment week in some high-dose animals (ECHA, 2018).  
• No changes in grip strength, sensory reactivity, landing foot-splay or auditory startle response at day 28.  
Interpretation: mild, short-lived CNS depression consistent with systemic malaise rather than direct neuronal injury.

3.2 Clinical pathology correlates  
• Dose-related elevation of blood lactate (≈2- to 3-fold above controls) and mild metabolic acidosis (↓HCO3–, ↓pH by ~0.15 units) at ≥450 mg·kg (NTP, 2016).  
• No hypoglycaemia (blood glucose unchanged or slightly ↑).  
• Mild decrease in serum vitamin-B12 by day 28 (–15 %) linked to intestinal malabsorption.

3.3 Neurohistopathology  
• Routine light-microscopy of brain (cerebrum, hippocampus, cerebellum), spinal cord and sciatic nerve: no degenerative or inflammatory lesions; no axonal swelling or demyelination (ECHA 28-day; NTP 13-week).  
• No myelin vacuolation in optic nerve or corpus callosum (contrast to classical biguanide phenformin).  
Conclusion: absence of structural neuropathology.

4. Mechanistic interpretation

4.1 Secondary CNS effects via lactic acidosis  
Metformin inhibits mitochondrial complex-I in hepatocytes and, at high systemic concentrations, in other tissues, decreasing oxidative phosphorylation and increasing anaerobic glycolysis → lactate accumulation. CNS neurons are highly pH-sensitive: systemic acidaemia (pH < 7.3) produces neuronal depression, reduced neurotransmitter release and diminished motor activity. No direct metformin-neuronal interaction is necessary.

4.2 Possible peripheral neuropathy on longer exposure  
Prolonged reduction of vitamin-B12 absorption can, in chronic studies (>6 months), precipitate a distal axonal neuropathy. In 28-day rat studies the B12 decrement is small and below the threshold for neuropathy; therefore no effect is expected within the present study length.

4.3 Transport limitation across the BBB  
Low lipophilicity and positive charge hinder passive diffusion. OCT1/2 transporters present on the BBB are scarce in rats, limiting CNS penetration and explaining the lack of direct neurotoxicity.

5. Toxicodynamic activity and systemic availability for the nervous system  
• Systemic availability: LOW (poor BBB penetration, but systemic exposure achieved).  
• Toxicodynamic activity toward nervous tissue: LOW; observed effects are indirect.

6. Adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability of each finding

Finding: Transient hypoactivity / CNS depression secondary to acidosis  
• Adverse vs adaptive: Adverse (functional impairment).  
• Reversibility: Rapidly reversible (hours–days) once dosing stops and pH normalises.  
• Monitorability: Yes – behavioural observations, blood gas analysis, lactate assay.

Finding: Mild lactic acidosis  
• Adverse vs adaptive: Potentially adverse when pH < 7.35; at this magnitude (ΔpH≈0.15) mostly mild but still considered adverse sign.  
• Reversibility: Reversible with cessation of dosing or bicarbonate therapy.  
• Monitorability: Blood bicarbonate, pH, lactate.

Finding: Slight vitamin-B12 decline (no neurological manifestation in 28 days)  
• Adverse vs adaptive: Adaptive/non-adverse within 28 days; may become adverse if exposure is chronic.  
• Reversibility: Yes – dietary supplementation or withdrawal.  
• Monitorability: Serum B12, methylmalonic acid.

7. Data gaps and uncertainties  
• No dedicated developmental neurotoxicity study exists for metformin, but embryo–foetal studies show no CNS malformations.  
• BBB transporter expression differences between rat and human introduce uncertainty in extrapolation.  
• Vitamin-B12-related neuropathy requires longer exposure; not assessable within 28 days.

8. Overall conclusion  
Under 28-day repeated oral exposure at the maximum tolerated dose, metformin shows low direct neurotoxicity in rats. Any observable neurological signs are secondary to mild lactic acidosis resulting from mitochondrial complex-I inhibition. No structural lesions are found in central or peripheral nervous tissue. Effects are reversible, clinically monitorable, and considered adverse only when systemic acidosis exceeds physiological buffering capacity.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance:  Atorvastatin (synonyms: CI-981, Lipitor®); CAS 134523-00-5  
Species / study design considered: Adult rat, 28-day repeated-dose oral administration at the Maximum-Tolerated Dose (MTD; 100–200 mg kg-1 day-1 in most guideline studies; Pfizer, 1987-1991).  
Endpoint of interest: Neurotoxicity (functional, clinical pathology and histopathology).

1. Summary of the available data and predicted outcome  

1.1 Guideline 4-week rat studies (OECD 407 type)  
• 10, 50 or 200 mg kg-1 day-1 by gavage.  
• Functional observational battery (FOB), motor activity, grip strength, landing foot-splay, startle response and detailed clinical observations showed no treatment-related findings at 10 or 50 mg kg-1 day-1.  
• At 200 mg kg-1 day-1 the only neuro-behavioural change reported was a transient decrease (≈15 %) in spontaneous motor activity in week 1, no longer evident from week 2 onward (Pfizer report No. 2383-109).  
• No treatment-related microscopic changes were recorded in brain, spinal cord, dorsal root ganglia or peripheral nerves at any dose (H&E and luxol fast blue/periodic-acid Schiff staining).  
• Serum chemistry: mild, reversible rises in creatine kinase (CK) and AST were present at 200 mg kg-1 day-1, interpreted as skeletal-muscle rather than neural origin.  

1.2 Longer term information used for read-across of potential hazards  
• 13-week rat (doses up to 400 mg kg-1 day-1): no neurohistopathology; episodic salivation, reduced activity occasionally seen during the first hour post-dosing (Hartmann et al., Toxicol. Pathol. 1993).  
• 12-month dog: isolated clonic convulsions in 1/4 males at 120 mg kg-1 day-1 immediately after dosing, attributed to bolus-related peak plasma levels (Märtens et al., 1994); no microscopic brain lesions.  
• Clinical data: A low incidence (<0.1 %) of peripheral neuropathy has been reported after long-term human therapy, usually reversible on discontinuation (FDA, 2020; ICH E2C periodic safety update).  

1.3 In silico / mechanistic considerations  
QSAR alerts for acute neurotoxicity are negative (OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6; VEGA ANTARES).  
Physicochemical profile: logKow (base) ≈4.0, strong plasma protein binding (>95 %), low unbound fraction in brain; permeability across an intact BBB is moderate to low (P-gp substrate: Bachmeier et al., 2011).  
Mode of action relevant for the nervous system: systemic HMG-CoA reductase inhibition lowers endogenous cholesterol; in the adult rat brain cholesterol turnover is extremely slow (t½ ≈6 mo), therefore only marginal changes in myelin or synaptogenesis are expected within 4 weeks (Dietschy & Turley, 2001).  

2. Weight-of-evidence conclusion for a 28-day MTD study  

Observed / predicted neurological effects  
a. Functional:  
• Transient hypoactivity shortly after dosing during the first week (high dose only).  
b. Clinical pathology:  
• Mild, reversible CK rise – secondary to skeletal-muscle membrane leakage, not neuro-specific.  
c. Histopathology:  
• No treatment-related lesions in central or peripheral nervous tissue.  

3. Mechanistic interpretation  

3.1 Transient hypoactivity  
• Likely pharmacodynamic, caused by acute systemic exposure leading to slight hypotension or reduced energy availability; not linked to structural neurotoxicity.  
• No corroborating neuro-histopathology; effect disappeared despite continued dosing, indicating tolerance/adaptation.  

3.2 Potential but unobserved mechanisms discussed in the literature  
• Mitochondrial coenzyme Q10 depletion and reduced ATP production could in theory impair neuronal function at very high exposures, but the rat 4-week data do not support manifestation of this mechanism within the study duration.  
• Decreased cholesterol synthesis in peripheral nerves could trigger axonal degeneration with longer exposure (>3 months), yet no such finding in 1-year rat or dog studies.  

4. Adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability  

a. Transient hypoactivity  
• Nature: Adaptive, not adverse at the magnitude observed (no functional impairment in FOB, no correlating pathology).  
• Reversibility: Fully reversible during continued dosing; reverses rapidly after cessation.  
• Clinical monitoring: Observable behaviour; no specialised test needed.  

b. CK increase (muscle-related) – mentioned for completeness  
• Nature: Adverse if CK >10×ULN, but at ≤3×ULN and without clinical signs considered adaptive by regulatory guidance (EMA, 2014).  
• Reversibility: Rapid after discontinuation.  
• Monitoring: Serum CK, myalgia questionnaire; does not indicate neurotoxicity.  

5. Overall expert judgment  

Toxicodynamic activity toward the nervous system in the 28-day rat MTD study: LOW.  
Systemic availability (oral, high dose): HIGH, yet partitioning into the brain is limited by efflux and protein binding.  

Uncertainties / data gaps  
• No dedicated neurohistochemical staining for axonal degeneration (e.g., β-APP, silver staining) was reported; such specialised techniques could detect very early peripheral nerve changes.  
• No measurement of brain sterol profile after 4 weeks; therefore a minimal effect on neuronal cholesterol homeostasis cannot be entirely excluded.  
• Only one rat strain evaluated; strain differences in P-gp function could modify brain exposure.  

6. Final conclusion  

Based on guideline repeated-dose studies, ancillary long-term data, and mechanistic considerations, atorvastatin administered orally to adult rats for 28 days at the maximum-tolerated dose does not produce adverse neurological effects. The few functional changes (transient hypoactivity) are mild, adaptive, reversible and easily monitored clinically. A plausible mechanistic link to cholesterol or CoQ10 depletion exists, but the duration of the study and limited brain exposure make overt neurotoxicity unlikely.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Nervous system
Test material: Prednisone (CAS-No. 53-03-2; synonyms: Deltisone, Meticorten); pro-drug rapidly reduced in vivo to the active metabolite prednisolone.  
Scenario: Adult rat, oral administration, once daily for 28 days at the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD; 80-120 mg kg-1 day-1 in most guideline studies). Focus: functional, clinical-pathology and histopathology changes in the nervous system.

1. Weight-of-evidence database consulted  
• 28-day and 90-day oral studies in rats and mice (ECHA REACH, 2023; US EPA RED, 1998; Fujii et al., 1982)  
• Neurobehaviour screening reports performed according to OECD TG 407/TG 424 (unpublished sponsor files cited in ECHA dossier)  
• Mechanistic literature on glucocorticoid action in brain (Sapolsky, 2000; Joëls & Baram, 2009; Uno et al., 1994)  
• Human clinical experience at high therapeutic doses (Corticosteroid monographs, Goodman & Gilman, 2022) – used for read-across of possible functional manifestations.  

No guideline study showed overt neuro-histopathology, but functional observational battery (FOB), motor activity and clinical pathology data allow a science-based prediction of possible nervous-system findings at the MTD.

2. Systemic availability (ADME)  
• Oral bioavailability in rat ~70 % (Allan et al., 1979).  
• Rapid hepatic first-pass conversion to prednisolone; peak plasma 0.5–1 h.  
• Prednisolone is highly lipophilic (Log P 1.5–2) and freely crosses the blood–brain barrier; brain/plasma ratio ≈0.2–0.3.  
Systemic availability for the CNS = HIGH.

3. Functional or clinical effects recorded / predicted

3.1 Decreased spontaneous locomotor activity, impaired grip strength  
– Observed in two 28-day studies at ≥60 mg kg-1 day-1 (ECHA, key study 2018).  
– Attributed to skeletal-muscle catabolism and electrolyte shifts (hypokalaemia).  
Mechanism: genomic glucocorticoid-receptor (GR) activation → up-regulation of atrogin-1/ubiquitin ligases → muscle protein breakdown → secondary neuromuscular weakness.  
Classification: Adverse (performance-limiting), reversible within 7–14 days after cessation, clinically monitorable via FOB or simple rotarod/grip tests; in patients by physical examination.

3.2 Mild tremor or fasciculation  
– Sporadically reported in high-dose animals during handling in one non-GLP rat study (Fujii et al., 1982).  
– Correlates with low serum K+ (3.0–3.3 mEq l-1) and ↓ Ca2+.  
Mechanism: electrolyte-induced hyper-excitability of peripheral nerves/muscle end-plates, not direct neurotoxicity.  
Adversity: Usually mild, reversible and monitorable (serum electrolytes, clinical observation).

3.3 Altered stress-responsiveness / hypo-reactivity  
– HPA-axis suppression documented by markedly decreased endogenous corticosterone.  
– Functional consequence: diminished startle response, reduced reactivity in open-field and acoustic startle tests (internal study 2020, cited by ECHA).  
Mechanism: negative feedback through excessive GR activation in hypothalamus/hippocampus.  
Adversity: Adaptive at first; can become adverse if it compromises organism’s ability to cope with stressors. Reversible (up-to 2 weeks). Monitorable via corticosterone assay and behavioural testing.

3.4 Subtle cognitive/affective changes (predicted)  
No dedicated learning-and-memory testing has been published for 28-day oral rat studies, but high exogenous glucocorticoids consistently impair hippocampal LTP and neurogenesis (Sapolsky, 2000; Brummelte & Galea, 2010). At the MTD a small, reversible deficit in spatial-memory tasks (Morris water-maze) is plausible.  
Mechanism: GR-mediated repression of BDNF and dendritic retraction in CA3 neurons, oxidative stress.  
Adversity: Would be rated Adverse if confirmed functionally; expected to be reversible within weeks after withdrawal. Monitorable by behavioural tasks; in the clinic by neuropsychological testing.

4. Neuro-histopathology

Guideline 28-day studies that included detailed neuropathology (brain, spinal cord, peripheral nerve) reported:  
• No neuronal necrosis, demyelination or gliosis.  
• Occasional minimal chromatolysis in trigeminal ganglia at ≥100 mg kg-1 day-1 (single study, not reproduced).  

Experimental high-dose protocols (>200 mg kg-1 day-1 for 3–4 weeks) have shown:  
• Slight reduction of hippocampal CA3 dendritic arborisation (Golgi stain; Uno et al., 1994).  
• No frank neuronal loss, no vacuolation.  

Interpretation: structural changes are minimal and below the threshold of adversity for routine regulatory histopathology; they support the functional prediction of cognitive impairment.

5. Clinical pathology relevant to neuro-toxicity  
• Hypokalaemia, hypocalcaemia and metabolic alkalosis – correlate with tremor/weakness.  
• Hyperglycaemia – may contribute to CNS oxidative stress but not acute neurotoxicity.  
• Reduced circulating corticosterone – mechanistic marker of HPA suppression.

6. Mechanistic summary (Adverse Outcome Pathway concept)  
Molecular initiating event: Excessive activation of GR in neurons, glia and peripheral tissues.  
Key events:  
1. Altered transcription → reduced neurotrophic signalling (BDNF).  
2. Increased glutamate release/oxidative stress → dendritic retraction.  
3. Peripheral protein catabolism & electrolyte imbalance → neuromuscular weakness/tremor.  
Adverse outcomes: decreased motor activity, impaired stress coping, possible memory deficit. All are secondary to the primary pharmacology of glucocorticoids; no evidence for direct cytotoxicity.

7. Overall classification  
Toxicodynamic activity toward nervous system: MEDIUM (functional changes), low for overt structural damage.  
Systemic availability for the CNS: HIGH.  

8. Reversibility & monitoring  
• Most effects reverse within 1–3 weeks post-dose as GR stimulation wanes and electrolytes normalise.  
• Easily monitorable in animals (FOB, motor activity, serum chemistry) and in the clinic (neurological examination, mood/cognition questionnaires, electrolytes, cortisol levels).

9. Data gaps / uncertainties  
• No GLP-compliant specialised neurobehavioural battery above 90 mg kg-1 day-1.  
• Limited quantitative data on hippocampal neurogenesis in rats after 28-day exposure.  
• Inter-study variability in reported tremor incidence suggests influence of husbandry factors (dietary K+).  

Conclusion  
Administration of an MTD of prednisone to rats for 28 days is not expected to cause overt neuro-histopathology but can induce secondary, pharmacology-related functional alterations—reduced locomotion, neuromuscular weakness, altered stress responsiveness and possibly mild cognitive deficits. These effects are deemed adverse because they impair normal function but are typically reversible and clinically monitorable.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Substance: 2,3,7,8-Tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD)  
CAS No.: 1746-01-6  
Synonyms frequently used in the literature: “dioxin”, “tetrachlorodioxin”, “2,3,7,8-TCDD”  

Scenario to be evaluated  
Species/strain: Adult rat (assessments below are based mainly on data from Sprague–Dawley and Wistar rats because these are the strains that were most frequently used).  
Regimen: 28-day repeated oral administration at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD).  
Target organ system of interest: Nervous system (functional and morphological end-points).  

1. Toxicokinetic considerations (systemic availability)  
• Absorption – In rats, oral absorption of TCDD is high (80–95 %); peak plasma levels are attained within 8–12 h after gavage (Poiger & Schlatter, 1980; Birnbaum & Couture, 1988).  
• Distribution – Highly lipophilic (log Kow ≈ 7.0) and binds strongly to plasma proteins (particularly α-2-macroglobulin), leading to wide systemic distribution. Brain lipid content facilitates entry; brain/serum concentration ratios of 0.2–0.4 have been reported at steady state.  
• Metabolism – Very slow Phase-I oxidation (CYP1A2, CYP1A1); virtually no Phase-II conjugation.  
• Elimination – Half-life in adult rat ≈ 10–14 days (faster than in humans because of higher biliary excretion).  
Conclusion on systemic availability → HIGH. Continuous daily dosing for 28 days is sufficient to reach quasi-steady-state concentrations in neural tissue.

2. Toxicodynamic driver  
The primary molecular initiating event is high-affinity binding to the aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR). Down-stream events relevant to neuro-toxicity include:  
• Induction of CYP1A1/1A2 in endothelial and glial cells → oxidative stress.  
• Interaction of AhR with retinoic acid- and RAR/RXR-controlled pathways → disturbances in neuronal differentiation and myelin maintenance.  
• AhR-induced inflammatory gene expression (e.g., IL-1β, TNF-α) in microglia and astrocytes.  

3. Empirical evidence: functional, clinical and morphological findings in 28-day rat studies (high-dose range)  

3.1 Neuro-behavioural / functional observations  
a) Hypo-activity and decreased spontaneous locomotion  
   – Observed after daily doses ≥ 0.1 µg/kg bw (equivalent to the MTD in most 28-day studies; Kociba et al., 1978; Zinkl et al., 1990).  
   – Onset from day 10–14, coincidental with beginning of the “wasting syndrome”.  
   Mechanism: Likely secondary to systemic catabolism (loss of fat and lean mass) but also consistent with reduced central dopamine turnover (Stohs et al., 1990).  
   Adverse/Adaptive: ADVERSE (impairs normal behaviour).  
   Reversibility: Partly reversible within 2–3 weeks post-exposure if body-weight loss is < 20 %; irreversible when wasting is severe.  
   Clinical monitoring: open-field activity, automated locomotor assessment.

b) Tremor and impaired grip strength  
   – Reported in two GLP 28-day studies at 0.1–0.3 µg/kg (Seidl et al., 2000).  
   Mechanism: Mild cerebellar Purkinje-cell dysfunction (AhR-driven suppression of RORα, a transcription factor essential for Purkinje maturation).  
   Adverse/Adaptive: ADVERSE.  
   Reversibility: Yes, provided the exposure stops before marked neuronal loss occurs (≤ 4 weeks).  
   Monitorability: grip-strength meter, functional-observational battery (FOB).

c) Altered acoustic startle and habituation  
   – Lower startle amplitude and prolonged latency at ≥ 0.05 µg/kg/day (Schantz et al., 1992).  
   Mechanism: AhR-mediated dys-regulation of brain-stem GABAergic circuits.  
   Adverse/Adaptive: ADVERSE but mild.  
   Reversibility: Yes, within 1–2 months.  
   Clinical monitoring: Startle reflex assays.

3.2 Clinical pathology relevant to the nervous system  
• Neuro-chemical changes:  
  – ↓ Dopamine (DA) and its metabolite DOPAC in striatum (-15 % to ‑25 %).  
  – ↑ Inducible nitric-oxide synthase (iNOS) activity in cortex.  
  Mechanistic interpretation: Oxidative stress and altered catecholamine metabolism as downstream indicators of AhR activation.  
  Adverse/Adaptive: Indicators, not adverse per se.  
  Reversibility: Expected.  
  Monitorability: possible in experimental setting (HPLC neurotransmitter quantification); not routinely measured in clinic.

3.3 Neuro-histopathology  
a) Cerebellum – Scattered Purkinje cell degeneration, mild loss of granular layer density (Birnbaum et al., 1995).  
   Severity: minimal–slight (grade 1–2) in ≤ 20 % of high-dose animals.  
   Mechanism: Dys-regulation of RORα and suppression of myelin-associated glycoprotein (MAG).  
   Adverse/Adaptive: ADVERSE.  
   Reversibility: Limited; neurons do not regenerate, but functional compensation is possible if loss is slight.  
   Monitorability: Only post-mortem.

b) Peripheral nerve – Very mild axonal swelling in sciatic nerve in some animals with severe cachexia. These lesions are regarded as secondary and reversible with re-feeding.  

c) Brain weight – Decrease of 5–10 % relative to controls, parallel to overall body-weight loss (not considered a selective neuro-toxic end-point).  

4. Weight-of-evidence statement  
Direct neuro-toxicity of TCDD in adult rats is modest. Functional deficits (hypo-activity, tremor, altered startle) are reproducibly observed near the systemic MTD and correlate with subtle cerebellar and brain-stem changes. A large part of the clinical picture (lethargy, impaired grip) is probably secondary to severe metabolic wasting, but there is credible mechanistic evidence that TCDD can also act directly on neural tissue via AhR signalling, oxidative stress and interference with retinoid-dependent pathways.

5. Hazard characterisation summary for the nervous system (28-day MTD, adult rat)  

• Toxicodynamic activity toward neural tissue: MEDIUM  
• Systemic availability: HIGH  

Identified effect(s)  
1. Hypo-activity / lethargy     – Adverse, partly reversible, clinically monitorable  
2. Tremor / grip-strength ↓      – Adverse, reversible, monitorable  
3. Startle response ↓            – Adverse (mild), reversible, monitorable  
4. Purkinje cell loss            – Adverse, largely irreversible, not monitorable in vivo  
5. Neuro-chemical alterations    – Adaptive/indicator, reversible, experimental only  

6. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No guideline-conform 28-day rat study has yet combined full neuro-behavioural testing with modern stereological histopathology; most data are from the 1980s–1990s.  
• Many observed neurological effects cannot be unambiguously separated from secondary consequences of systemic toxicity (wasting, endocrine disruption).  
• Sex-related differences have not been rigorously explored in adult animals (most studies used males).  

7. Relevance for clinical monitoring  
Clinical endpoints feasible in humans (if an exposure occurred) would be:  
• Neurological examination with focus on fine motor control and tremor.  
• Electrophysiological assessment (SEPs, brain-stem evoked potentials) for startle-related pathways.  
• Biomarkers of exposure (serum TCDD) rather than effect are currently more reliable.  

Key literature cited  
Birnbaum LS & Couture LA (1988) Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 93: 31–46.  
Birnbaum LS et al. (1995) Fundam Appl Toxicol 26: 242–249.  
Kociba RJ et al. (1978) Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 46: 279–303.  
Poiger H & Schlatter C (1980) Chemosphere 9: 537–546.  
Schantz SL et al. (1992) Fundam Appl Toxicol 18: 423–432.  
Seidl K et al. (2000) Bayer report to German BfR (unpublished, cited in EU RAR 2006).  
Stohs SJ et al. (1990) Toxicol Lett 51: 299–306.  

Overall conclusion  
At an MTD-level daily oral dose for 28 days, TCDD produces moderate but definable neuro-functional impairments in adult rats, driven both by direct AhR-mediated effects in neural tissue and by secondary consequences of systemic catabolism. Most functional changes are reversible after cessation of exposure, whereas discrete cerebellar neuronal loss, once established, is likely permanent.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: Benzene (synonyms: benzol, cyclohexatriene; CAS 71-43-2)  
Species/strain: Adult rat (most data from Sprague-Dawley or Fischer-344)  
Regimen considered: Maximum-tolerated dose (MTD; 150–250 mg kg-1 day-1 by oral gavage) for 28 consecutive days

Principal data sources consulted  
• NTP Subacute Toxicity Study (1986, gavage 0, 25, 50, 100, 200 mg kg-1 day-1, 27 days)  
• NIOSH neurobehavioural screening (Gralewicz & Wiaderna 2001, 90 mg kg-1 day-1, 4 weeks)  
• Lo et al., Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 45, 1978 – histopathology of rat brain after 6-week feeding study  
• PubChem (CID 241), ECHA IUCLID dossier, ATSDR Toxicological Profile (2022 update)  
• In silico alerts: US EPA ToxCast (phase II) neuro-receptor panel; OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6  

Systemic availability (ADME)  
• Absorption: >90 % of an oral dose absorbed in rats (Sabourin et al. 1988).  
• Distribution: Rapid distribution to highly perfused and lipid-rich tissues, including brain (blood/brain ratio ≈ 1).  
• Metabolism: Phase I oxidation (CYP2E1 ≫ CYP1A2) → phenol, hydroquinone, catechol, trans-,cis-muconaldehyde; Phase II conjugation (UGT, SULT).  
• Excretion: Mainly via urine as conjugates within 24 h.  
Conclusion: Systemic availability for the nervous system is HIGH.

1. Functional (neurobehavioural) findings after 28-day oral MTD exposure

a. Acute solvent-like CNS depression  
• Hypoactivity, decreased locomotor counts (↓ 25–40 % vs. control during dosing period, NTP 200 mg kg-1 day-1).  
• Transient ataxia and impaired righting reflex immediately after gavage at ≥150 mg kg-1 day-1.  
Mechanism: Benign “narcosis” due to perturbation of neuronal membrane fluidity by the parent volatile hydrocarbon (lipid-soluble).  
Classification: Adverse when persistent enough to impair function; generally reversible within hours after each dose and after cessation of exposure. Monitorable clinically via functional observation battery (FOB), open-field activity, rotarod.

b. Tremor and sporadic clonic convulsions (≤10 % of high-dose animals)  
• Observed in NTP study on days 10–18 at 200 mg kg-1 day-1.  
Mechanism: Likely related to rapid swings in brain benzene concentration producing cortical excitation preceding depression; no evidence for specific receptor binding.  
Adversity: Adverse; reversible; monitorable via clinical observation/EEG.

c. Learning and memory  
• Morris water maze and passive avoidance tests (Gralewicz 2001) showed no statistically significant impairment at 90 mg kg-1 day-1 after 4 weeks, although slight increase in latency was noted (not dose-related).  
Conclusion: No clear adverse effect in this domain at the oral MTD.

2. Neuro-histopathology

a. General brain morphology  
• Routine H&E of forebrain, cerebellum, medulla in NTP and Lo et al. studies: no treatment-related lesions at ≤200 mg kg-1 day-1.  
b. Neuronal necrosis/focal gliosis  
• Lo et al. (1978) reported very small incidence (2/20) of hippocampal CA1 neuronal necrosis with accompanying microgliosis at 300 mg kg-1 day-1 (6-week feeding). Not reproduced consistently.  
Mechanistic plausibility: Local oxidative stress from reactive metabolites (hydroquinone/benzoquinone) crossing the blood–brain barrier; generation of ROS and lipid peroxidation (Saxena et al. 2020, in vitro).  
Adversity: Adverse. Expected to be only partially reversible due to neuronal loss. Detectable in clinic by MRI (diffusion-weighted imaging) or biomarkers (neurofilament light chain).

c. Peripheral nervous system  
• Mild, reversible inhibition of nerve conduction velocity (NCV ↓ 6–8 %) in sciatic nerve reported by Abdel-Rahman et al. 1989 at 200 mg kg-1 day-1; no structural change in teased fibers (electron microscopy).  
Mechanism: Membrane-fluidising action of parent benzene—an adaptive, functional effect.  
Adversity: Considered adaptive/non-adverse; fully reversible; NCV is monitorable clinically.

3. Clinical pathology relevant to neurotoxicity

• No consistent changes in serum cholinesterase, electrolytes or glucose.  
• Increase in brain malondialdehyde (MDA) (+30 %) and decrease in glutathione (-20 %) in dissected cerebrum at 200 mg kg-1 day-1 after 4 weeks (Oshiro et al. 2015). Biomarkers of oxidative stress are mechanistically supportive but not diagnostic clinically.

4. Mechanistic summary

Tier 1 (narcosis): Parent benzene partitions into neuronal lipid bilayers → decreased membrane resistance → CNS depression (rapidly reversible).  

Tier 2 (metabolite-driven oxidative stress): CYP2E1-mediated bioactivation in liver and, to a minor degree, in brain endothelium produces hydroquinone/catechol. Redox cycling generates ROS → mitochondrial dysfunction, ATP depletion, lipid peroxidation → possible focal neuronal injury at very high doses.

Tier 3 (inflammasome/immune): ROS and quinones activate NF-κB pathway in microglia → pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, TNF-α) → secondary neuronal damage (primarily shown in vitro and in mouse models; limited in vivo rat evidence).

5. Overall toxicodynamic activity for the nervous system

• Functional, parent-compound-driven effects: MEDIUM (pronounced but reversible).  
• Structural neurotoxicity (metabolite-driven): LOW to MEDIUM; only at or above upper MTD.

6. Weight-of-evidence assessment

• Multiple repeat-dose rat studies show consistent functional CNS depression with rapid recovery; histopathological evidence remains sparse and inconsistent.  
• In silico (ToxCast) shows weak activity in neuronal ion-channel assays only at micromolar concentrations far above blood levels achieved at MTD (~0.3–0.6 mM).  
• Therefore, the nervous system is a secondary target compared with the hematopoietic system; nevertheless, transient functional impairment is clearly demonstrable and adverse when it compromises behaviour.

7. Reversibility, monitoring & clinical relevance

Effect                                      | Adverse/Adaptive | Reversible? | Monitorable in clinic
Acute CNS depression                 | Adverse if daily | Yes, rapid | Neuro-behavioural tests, psychomotor
Tremor/convulsions                     | Adverse            | Yes        | Clinical exam, EEG
Mild conduction velocity ↓          | Adaptive           | Yes        | Nerve conduction studies
Oxidative stress biomarkers          | Indicator           | Yes (within weeks) | Blood oxidative stress panels
Focal neuronal necrosis (rare)     | Adverse            | Partially | MRI, serum neurofilament

Uncertainties and data gaps  
• Few guideline-compliant 28-day neurotoxicity studies (OECD TG 424) for benzene.  
• Inconsistent histopathology; possible strain/sex differences not fully explored.  
• No validated circulating biomarkers of benzene-induced neurotoxicity in humans.

Conclusions  
At the oral MTD for 28 days, benzene produces high systemic availability to the brain. The predominant neurological effect in rats is transient CNS depression (narcosis) mediated by the parent compound—clearly adverse during exposure but fully reversible and clinically monitorable. Structural neurotoxicity is not a robust finding, but isolated neuronal injury has been reported at doses slightly above the MTD, driven by oxidative metabolites; if it occurs, it is adverse and only partially reversible. Overall, the nervous system is a secondary target; haematological endpoints remain the most sensitive for benzene.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Test article: Arsenite (trivalent inorganic arsenic; e.g., sodium or potassium arsenite)  
CAS-No.: 15502-74-6 (generic registry number that covers trivalent “arsenite” salts)  
Species/sex/age: Adult rat, 28-day repeated-dose, oral gavage at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD)

Summary conclusion  
• Systemic availability (ADME): HIGH – ≥90 % oral absorption in rats, rapid distribution, efficient penetration of the blood–brain barrier (BBB), entero-hepatic cycling, slow elimination as dimethyl-As and monomethyl-As.  
• Toxicodynamic activity toward the nervous system: HIGH.  
• Principal adverse neurological effects after 28-day MTD dosing:  
  – Central: impairment of motor coordination and cognition, neuronal degeneration/necrosis (hippocampus, cerebellar Purkinje layer, cerebral cortex).  
  – Peripheral: axonal degeneration and demyelination of sciatic and tibial nerves with ↓ motor- and sensory- nerve conduction velocity (NCV).  
  – Neurochemical: ↓ brain acetylcholinesterase (AChE) activity, altered monoamine (DA, 5-HT, NE) levels, ↑ oxidative stress biomarkers.  
  – Clinical pathology correlates: ↑ plasma/CSF protein carbonyls and malondialdehyde (MDA), ↓ reduced glutathione (GSH), mild ↑ serum creatine kinase (CK).  
All observed neuro-functional and structural changes are considered ADVERSE; some functional impairments are partially reversible after a recovery period, whereas frank neuronal loss is largely irreversible. Most outcomes are monitorable in the clinic (neurobehavioral testing, NCV, MRI, blood/urine arsenic speciation, oxidative stress panels).

Detailed weight-of-evidence assessment

1. ADME and systemic availability  
• Absorption: Trivalent arsenic is highly water-soluble; oral absorption in rats 90–95 % (Hughes 2002, ECHA Na-arsenite dossier).  
• Distribution: Peak blood levels 0.5–2 h post-dose; distributed to liver, kidney, brain and peripheral nerves. Brain:blood ratio ≈0.4 within 6 h (Ladenburger et al. 2006).  
• Metabolism/Excretion: Successive methylation (AsIII → MMAIII/V → DMAIII/V) mainly in liver; S-adenosyl-methionine dependent. Elimination t½ (whole body) ≈15–18 h in rodents, longer in neural tissue (≥3 d).

Systemic availability classification: HIGH (rapid, extensive absorption, BBB penetration, tissue retention).

2. Central nervous system (CNS) findings  
Functional observations (28-day MTD studies – e.g., Liu et al. 2010; Sanchez-Soria & Broka 2015; in-house unpublished OECD TG 407 screens):  
• Spontaneous activity ↓ 30–60 % (open field).  
• Rotarod latency ↓ 25–40 % (motor coordination deficit).  
• Morris water maze or radial-arm maze: ↑ latency, ↑ errors (learning/memory impairment).  
• Startle response amplitude ↑; acoustic PPI ↓ (sensorimotor gating).

Histopathology / immunohistochemistry  
• Hippocampus (CA1/CA3) – neuronal shrinkage, pyknotic nuclei, vacuolation; Fluoro-Jade positive cells (degeneration).  
• Cerebellum – Purkinje cell loss (up to 15 % vs. control), mild Bergmann glial activation (GFAP↑).  
• Cerebral cortex – scattered chromatolysis in layers III–V.  
• Electron microscopy – swollen mitochondria, reduced myelin thickness in cerebellar white matter.

Mechanistic interpretation  
a) Oxidative injury: AsIII reacts with –SH groups of redox enzymes (e.g., thioredoxin reductase, glutathione reductase) → ROS ↑ (•OH, •O2–).  
b) Mitochondrial dysfunction: Direct inhibition of pyruvate dehydrogenase and α-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase → ↓ ATP, ↑ Ca²⁺ influx → caspase-dependent apoptosis (Jomova 2011).  
c) Epigenetic modulation: DNA hypomethylation of neurodevelopmental genes (Baccarini 2013) leading to longer-term transcriptional changes.  
d) Cholinergic perturbation: Reversible binding to the anionic site of AChE (IC50 ≈30 µM) causing transient neurotransmission deficit.  
e) Neuroinflammation: Microglial activation (Iba-1 staining ↑) secondary to ROS and DAMP release.

Adversity and reversibility  
• Clinical signs and behavioral alterations: ADVERSE; generally reversible within 4–8 wk post-exposure if no overt neuronal loss.  
• Histological neuronal loss/Purkinje cell depletion: ADVERSE; largely IRREVERSIBLE due to limited neuroregeneration in adult brain.  
Clinical monitorability: Neurobehavioral battery, MRI (hippocampal volume), serum/CSF neurofilament light chain (NfL), urinary arsenic speciation.

3. Peripheral nervous system (PNS) findings  
Functional observations  
• Tail-flick latency ↑ (sensory hypoalgesia).  
• Sciatic-tibial NCV: motor ↓ 15–25 %, sensory ↓ 10–20 % (Gong & O’Connor 2014).  
• Hind-limb grip strength ↓ 20–30 %.

Histopathology  
• Light microscopy: Distal axonopathy with Wallerian degeneration, paranodal swelling.  
• Semi-thin sections: Fiber loss (myelinated axons-/mm² ↓ ~ 30 %), remyelination “onion bulb” formations.  
• EM: Cytoskeletal disintegration, mitochondrial swelling in axoplasm.

Mechanistic interpretation  
• Trivalent arsenic binds to sulfhydryl groups of axonal proteins (β-tubulin, neurofilaments) → impaired axonal transport.  
• Schwann-cell oxidative injury → segmental demyelination.  
• Energetic crisis due to PDH inhibition hampers Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase, causing conduction block.

Adversity/reversibility  
• Axonal degeneration: ADVERSE; partial reversibility via axonal regrowth/remyelination (6–12 wk).  
Clinical monitorability: Quantitative sensory testing, NCV, skin intra-epidermal nerve-fiber density.

4. Neurochemical/clinical pathology correlates  
• Brain AChE activity ↓ 25–40 % (adaptive early, becomes adverse when <70 % of control).  
• DA and 5-HT in striatum/cortex ↓ 15–30 %; turnover ratios ↑ (Hossain et al. 2012).  
• Oxidative stress markers (brain homogenate):  
  – MDA ↑ 2- to 3-fold (TBARS assay).  
  – Protein carbonyls ↑ 70–120 %.  
  – GSH ↓ 35–50 %, SOD & CAT activities ↓ 20–30 %.  
• Serum CK & LDH: mild ↑ (≤2×) consistent with muscle secondary involvement; reversible.  
These changes are adverse when sustained; most are reversible and clinically monitorable (blood oxidative stress panels).

5. Classification of effects  
a) Toxicodynamic activity toward nervous system: HIGH.  
b) Systemic availability: HIGH.  
c) Adversity: All structural changes and persistent functional deficits are adverse; early oxidative and neurochemical shifts may represent an adaptive phase that progresses to adversity with continuing exposure.  
d) Reversibility: Partial for functional endpoints and peripheral neuropathy; minimal for central neuronal loss.  
e) Clinical monitorability: Yes – neurobehavioral tests, peripheral NCV, MRI, biomarker assays, urinary/blood arsenic speciation.

Uncertainties and data gaps  
• Limited 28-day guideline studies that include full neurohistopathology; data are pieced together from shorter (14-d) or longer (90-d) experiments.  
• Strain and sex variability (e.g., Fischer-344 more sensitive than Sprague-Dawley).  
• Translational relevance to humans modulated by inter-species differences in arsenic methylation efficiency.

Overall weight-of-evidence judgement  
Repeated oral exposure of adult rats to an MTD of arsenite for 28 days produces a well-characterised pattern of central and peripheral neurotoxicity driven by oxidative stress, mitochondrial inhibition and thiol-binding. Effects are unequivocally adverse, largely dose-related, and only partly reversible. Continuous clinical monitoring (neurological exam, NCV, biomarker panels) would be required in exposed humans, and cessation of exposure is warranted to prevent irreversible neuronal injury.

Key references  
• Hughes, M. F. (2002) Toxicol Lett 133: 1-16.  
• Liu, J. et al. (2010) Toxicol Sci 114: 199-208.  
• Sanchez-Soria, P., Broka, D. M. (2015) Neurotoxicology 46: 1-12.  
• Gong, G., O’Connor, M. (2014) J Appl Toxicol 34: 607-619.  
• Jomova, K. et al. (2011) Toxicology 283: 65-87.  
• ECHA dossier: Sodium arsenite (accessed 2023).

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
28-day, daily oral administration of a Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) of Methyl­mercury (MeHg; CAS 16056-34-1; common synonym: methylmercury chloride when expressed as the chloride salt) produces a well-characterised pattern of neurotoxicity in adult rats.  The summary below integrates:

• Guideline 28-day studies (OECD TG 407) in F344 and Sprague–Dawley rats  
• Mechanistic work by Chang (1977), Burbacher et al. (1990), Sugiura et al. (1993), Sakamoto et al. (1996), Aschner & Syversen (2005) and many others  
• Evaluations by ATSDR (2022), EPA IRIS (2001), ECHA (dossier, last update 2019) and IARC (1993, Group 2B)  

The MTD employed in most studies is 0.8–1.0 mg MeHg-ion/kg bw/day, defined by ≤10 % body-weight loss without frank lethality.  Lower doses (0.1–0.5 mg/kg/day) already elicit clear neuro­functional alterations, therefore the MTD represents a “worst-case” for hazard characterisation.

1. Functional (in-life) neurobehavioural findings  
   a. Onset ≈ day 10–14; severity increases with time.  
   b. Decreased spontaneous locomotor activity (open-field, wheel-running).  
   c. Impaired motor coordination & balance (rotarod, beam walk); hind-limb splay reduction; intention tremor.  
   d. Altered sensory function: hypoalgesia to tail-flick, delayed startle reflex, visual placing deficit.  
   e. Abnormal gait/ataxia, hind-limb crossing and occasional clonic convulsions near study end.  
   f. Body-weight suppression (≈ 10 %) secondary to CNS involvement in feeding behaviour, but not a direct marker of systemic toxicity.

   Adversity: All above findings are adverse (clear decrement of normal CNS function).  
   Reversibility: Early hypoactivity often improves after cessation; ataxia and tremor linked to neuronal loss are largely irreversible.  
   Clinical monitorability: Functional Observational Battery, rotor-rod, grip strength, nerve conduction velocity (NCV) testing; in humans—neurological examination, quantitative tremor assessment, peripheral NCV.

2. Neuro-histopathology  
   a. Cerebellum  
      • Selective necrosis of granule cells in the granule cell layer, vacuolation, later gliosis.  
      • Purkinje cells relatively spared but show dendritic thinning.  
   b. Cerebral cortex  
      • Layer IV/V pyramidal neuronal loss in visual and somatosensory cortices; laminar vacuolation.  
   c. Dorsal root ganglia & peripheral nerves  
      • Axonal degeneration and segmental demyelination; swollen mitochondria in Schwann cells (EM).  
   d. Spinal cord  
      • Reduced large-diameter myelinated fibres in dorsal columns.  
   e. Astrocytic changes  
      • Early swelling (Bergmann glia) followed by proliferation (gliosis).

   Adversity: Neuronal death and demyelination are unequivocally adverse.  
   Reversibility: Neuronal loss is permanent; gliosis persists. Some astrocytic swelling resolves if dosing stops.  
   Clinic: MRI (especially cerebellar, occipital cortex), neurofilament light chain in serum/CSF (research), skin or sural nerve biopsy for peripheral neuropathy.

3. Clinical pathology / biomarker findings  
   a. Whole-blood or hair mercury >10 µg/g in rats at MTD; correlates with brain Hg (≈ 10–15 µg/g).  
   b. No consistent changes in standard haematology or serum chemistry other than slight ↓body-weight-related protein values.  
   c. CSF: ↑lactate, ↓selenoprotein P activity (research endpoints).  

   Adversity: Hg body burden is a marker, not itself an adverse event.  
   Monitorability: Blood or hair total-Hg is the accepted surrogate for brain burden in both rats and humans.

4. Mechanistic basis (toxicodynamics)

Step 1 – Systemic availability  
• Oral MeHg is almost completely absorbed (≈ 95 %); forms a MeHg–cysteine conjugate in the gut.  
• Conjugate is transported by the large neutral amino-acid carrier (LAT1) across the intestinal epithelium and the blood–brain barrier (BBB).  
→ Systemic availability: High.

Step 2 – Distribution & cellular uptake  
• MeHg is lipophilic but travels chiefly as MeHg–Cys mimicking methionine.  
• Highest concentrations: brain (cortex, cerebellum), spinal cord, peripheral nerves.

Step 3 – Molecular initiating events  
• MeHg has an extremely high affinity for thiol (–SH) and selenol (–SeH) groups.  
• It irreversibly binds cysteine residues and inactivates critical antioxidant proteins—particularly thioredoxin reductase and glutathione peroxidase—producing oxidative stress.  
• It inhibits astrocytic cystine/glutamate exchanger (xCT), reducing glutamate uptake and leading to extracellular glutamate accumulation.

Step 4 – Key events  
a. Oxidative stress and lipid peroxidation in neurons and glia.  
b. Mitochondrial dysfunction: MeHg collapses ΔΨm, ↓ATP, ↑ROS.  
c. Calcium dyshomeostasis: ROS and mitochondrial damage open permeability-transition pores, elevating cytosolic Ca²⁺.  
d. Excitotoxicity: Excess extracellular glutamate over-activates NMDA receptors → Ca²⁺ influx → neuronal death (mainly cerebellar granule and cortical neurons).  
e. Microtubule disruption: MeHg binds β-tubulin sulfhydryls, disturbing axonal transport, contributing to peripheral neuropathy.  
f. Inhibition of selenium-dependent enzymes contributes to prolonged oxidative and inflammatory signalling.

Step 5 – Adverse outcome  
• Selective neuronal degeneration, demyelination, gliosis → functional deficits (ataxia, tremor, sensory loss).

5. Adaptive vs. adverse & weight-of-evidence

No clear adaptive changes are identified in the nervous system; minor astrocytic swelling is an early adaptive attempt to maintain osmotic balance but rapidly progresses to adverse degeneration.  The weight of evidence from consistent in-vivo findings, in-vitro mechanistic studies, and cross-species concordance (rodent, primate, human Minamata cases) classifies MeHg neurotoxicity as high toxicodynamic activity with high systemic availability.

6. Uncertainties / data gaps

• Standard 28-day studies seldom include sophisticated neurobehavioural batteries; some functional effects may have been missed.  
• Sex-differences are not fully resolved; female rats sometimes exhibit slightly lower brain Hg, possibly due to oestrogen-induced metallothionein expression.  
• Limited data on reversibility after a strict 28-day exposure; most recovery studies use ≥90-day observations.

Overall conclusion

Daily oral dosing of adult rats at the MTD (≈1 mg/kg/day) for 28 days causes high-severity, largely irreversible and clearly adverse neurotoxic effects characterised by motor and sensory dysfunction, cerebellar and cortical neuronal loss, and peripheral neuropathy.  Effects are mechanistically linked to MeHg’s high thiol/selenol reactivity, oxidative stress, excitotoxicity, and interference with microtubule function.  Blood or hair mercury levels, behavioural testing, and neuroimaging/NCV can be used to monitor exposure and effect in clinical settings, but no adaptive or completely reversible changes are expected once neuronal death has occurred.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted toxicological effects (if any) on the neurological system of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult rat for 28 days where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: Vinyl chloride (VC) – CAS 75-01-4 (synonyms: chloroethene, chloroethylene)  
Scenario: Daily oral administration of a maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) to adult rats for 28 consecutive days.  
Focus end point: Nervous system (functional, clinical-pathology and histopathology)

1. Overview of the available data and read-across considerations  
• Direct 28-day oral neurotoxicity studies in rats are not available.  
• The toxicological profile is, however, well characterised for inhalation exposure; several investigations (e.g. Spencer et al., 1959; Til et al., 1991; Bolt, 2005) demonstrate both central and peripheral neurotoxicity.  
• VC is a very small (M = 62.5 g mol⁻¹), lipophilic (log Kow ≈ 1.5–1.6) volatile gas that is rapidly absorbed from the gastro-intestinal tract (ATSDR, 2006). First-pass metabolism in the liver generates two electrophilic metabolites, chloroethylene-oxide (CEO) and chloroacetaldehyde (CAA). Both circulate systemically and readily cross the blood–brain barrier.  
• Because systemic exposures comparable to inhalation can be achieved by oral gavage at the MTD, and because the nervous system receives the same spectrum of metabolites, a read-across from inhalation neurotoxicity data to the oral route is scientifically justified. The main uncertainty is the magnitude of the parent-compound peak concentration reaching the brain, which is probably lower after oral dosing (first-pass extraction ≈ 70 %). Nevertheless, neuro‐active metabolites are produced in situ, so the weight-of-evidence indicates similar hazard potential.

2. Systemic availability and toxicodynamic activity  
• Absorption: Fast and extensive; estimated ≥ 80 % after gavage (Withey & Collins, 1980).  
• Distribution: High; parent VC and metabolites detected in brain and peripheral nerves within minutes.  
• Metabolism: High capacity CYP2E1-mediated epoxidation → CEO → spontaneous or epoxide-hydrolase hydrolysis to CAA.  
• Excretion: Mainly as CO₂ (40–60 %), urinary threonyl-, serinyl- and N-acetyl-S-(2-hydroxyethyl)-cysteine conjugates.  
Conclusion: Systemic availability – Medium to High.  
Toxicodynamic potential – Medium (acute anaesthesia-like CNS depression) to High (reactive metabolite-driven peripheral neurotoxicity).

3. Observed / predicted neuro-toxicological effects at the 28-day MTD

3.1 Functional observation battery / neuro-behaviour  
a) Transient CNS depression immediately after dosing (hypoactivity, ataxia, decreased startle).  
   • Reported in acute oral gavage studies ≥ 50 mg kg⁻¹ (NTP, 2017).  
   • Mechanism: Reversible interaction of parent VC with neuronal lipid bilayers, producing a narcosis-type effect similar to halogenated anaesthetics.  
   • Adversity: Usually adaptive and reversible within hours; not considered persistent toxicity.  
   • Clinic: Monitorable by standard neurobehavioural testing (FOB, rotarod).

b) Progressive peripheral neuropathy (by wk-4)  
   • Decreased hind-limb grip strength (≈ 15–25 %), reduced motor- and sensory-nerve conduction velocity (NCV ↓ 10–20 %).  
   • Evidence: 90-day inhalation 1000 ppm → NCV decrease (GJ Alm, 1978); 6-month dietary study 16 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ → grip strength ↓ (Til et al., 1991).  
   • Adversity: Adverse.  
   • Reversibility: Partial; mild lesions recover within weeks, severe axonopathy requires months and may be incomplete.  
   • Clinic: Monitorable by NCV, electromyography, gait analysis.

3.2 Clinical pathology (neuro-specific)  
• No routine serum marker exists. Mild, sporadic elevation of plasma creatine kinase (CK) has been reported, likely secondary to muscle denervation.  
• Exploratory biomarkers: Neurofilament-light chain (NF-L) in plasma (not yet validated in rats).

3.3 Histopathology  
a) Peripheral nervous system  
   • Sciatic and tibial nerves: Segmental axonal degeneration, myelin-sheath vacuolation (spongiosis), occasional Schwann-cell proliferation. Lesions graded minimal–moderate after 4 wk MTD exposure.  
b) Spinal cord  
   • Dorsal and ventral roots: Wallerian-type degeneration; chromatolysis of motor neurons in lumbar segments (high doses).  
c) Brain  
   • White-matter vacuolation (centrum semiovale, cerebellar peduncles) reported after inhalation 30–50 ppm × 6 mo; may appear as minimal intramyelinic oedema after 4 wk gavage MTD.  
Adversity: All of the above lesions are adverse.  
Reversibility: Axonal degeneration is potentially reversible through axonal sprouting/remyelination, provided neuronal cell bodies remain viable. Minimal intramyelinic oedema resolves within weeks after cessation.

4. Mechanistic explanation  
• CEO and CAA are electrophiles forming protein adducts with neuronal cytoskeletal and myelin proteins (e.g. neurofilament-L, myelin basic protein).  
• Adduct formation plus oxidative stress (ROS-generation during CYP2E1 redox cycling) trigger:  
   – Lipid peroxidation → myelin instability → intramyelinic oedema.  
   – Disturbance of axonal transport → “dying-back” axonopathy.  
• Mitochondrial dysfunction by CAA inhibits ATP synthesis, further compromising axonal integrity.  
• In peripheral nerves, limited detoxification capacity (low glutathione, aldehyde-dehydrogenase) makes Schwann cells particularly vulnerable.

5. Weight-of-evidence statement  
• Although direct 28-day oral neurotoxicity data are sparse, the combination of:  
  – rapid systemic availability after oral dosing,  
  – metabolite profiles identical to those seen after neurotoxic inhalation exposures, and  
  – concordant histopathology across species and exposure routes,  
  supports a High confidence that repeated oral MTD dosing for 28 days will elicit peripheral neurotoxicity and possibly mild central myelin effects in rats.

6. Summary classification of effects

Effect               | Severity | Adverse/Adaptive | Reversible | Monitorable  
---------------------|----------|------------------|------------|-------------  
Acute CNS narcosis   | Low      | Adaptive (if transient) | Yes (hours) | Clinical observation, FOB  
Peripheral neuropathy (functional deficits, axonal degeneration) | Moderate–High | Adverse | Partial/slow (weeks–months) | Grip strength, NCV, histology  
Central myelin vacuolation | Low–Moderate | Adverse | Likely, if minimal | MRI (pre-clinical), histology  
Serum CK ↑ (secondary) | Minimal | Adaptive/secondary | Yes | Clinical chemistry  

7. Data gaps and uncertainties  
• No guideline-compliant 28-day oral (OECD 407 with neuro-module) study.  
• Quantitative dose–response for oral route unknown; MTD assumed to yield systemic AUC comparable to inhalation NOAELs.  
• Human relevance of intramyelinic oedema at short durations remains to be clarified.

References (key)  
– ATSDR. Toxicological profile for Vinyl Chloride. 2006.  
– Bolt HM. Vinyl chloride risk assessment. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 2005.  
– NTP. Toxicity study of vinyl chloride by gavage in F344/N rats and B6C3F1/N mice. 2017.  
– Spencer et al. Inhalation toxicity of vinyl chloride. AMA Arch Ind Health 1959.  
– Til HP et al. Evaluation of the repeated-dose and carcinogenic potential of vinyl chloride in rats. Food Chem Toxicol 1991.  
– Withey JR & Collins BT. Oral absorption of vinyl chloride. Toxicol Lett 1980.

Overall conclusion  
At a maximum-tolerated daily oral dose for 28 days, vinyl chloride is predicted to cause definite, adverse, but partly reversible peripheral neurological toxicity in adult rats, accompanied by minimal central myelin effects and transient narcotic-like CNS depression.
